rationales.A: Continuing valproate is wrong because it is highly teratogenic (NTD, cardiac, craniofacial, cognitive impairment) and is contraindicated in women of reproductive age unless a TGA Pregnancy Prevention Programme is in place AND no alternative controls seizures; folate alone does not mitigate the teratogenic risk. Would be acceptable only when every alternative antiepileptic has failed and PPP criteria are fully met, never as routine preconception advice (RANZCOG; TGA valproate PPP; AMH).
rationales.C: Abrupt cessation is wrong because antiepileptic withdrawal risks status epilepticus, which carries higher maternal and foetal mortality than the drug itself; the anti-pattern is patient-driven cessation without neurology cover. Would be correct only inside a supervised neurology switch with overlap onto a safer agent, not as standalone advice (RANZCOG; AMH).
rationales.D: Adding lamotrigine on top of valproate is wrong because polytherapy multiplies teratogenic risk rather than reducing it, and valproate must be removed entirely whenever possible. Would be correct only as a transient bridge during a planned cross-titration off valproate under neurology, not as a maintenance plan for pregnancy (RANZCOG; AMH).
rationales.E: Folic acid 400 mcg is wrong because women on antiepileptics require the high-dose 5 mg regimen preconception until 12 weeks, and even adequate folate does not neutralise valproate teratogenicity. Would be correct as the dose for a low-risk woman not on antiepileptics; here both the dose and the drug choice need to change (RANZCOG; AMH).

rationales.B: Maternal antenatal oral vitamin K is wrong because the NHMRC Joint Statement and RANZCOG no longer recommend it for women on antiepileptic drugs; the evidence does not show a clinically meaningful reduction in neonatal haemorrhagic disease over standard neonatal IM vitamin K. Would be correct under the older RCOG-era practice that has now been superseded in Australia (RANZCOG; NHMRC Joint statement on vitamin K).
rationales.C: Switching valproate to lamotrigine at 34 weeks is wrong because organogenesis is complete and a third-trimester swap risks breakthrough seizures with no foetal benefit; the anti-pattern is delayed cross-titration once pregnancy is established. Would be correct only when planned preconception, not as a strategy to reduce neonatal bleeding (Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology).
rationales.D: Elective caesarean is wrong because mode of delivery does not alter the pathophysiology of vitamin K deficient bleeding, which is driven by transplacental enzyme induction by the antiepileptic. Would be appropriate only for standard obstetric indications, never as VKDB prophylaxis (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: FFP prophylaxis is wrong because it is reserved for neonates with active bleeding plus documented coagulopathy, carries transfusion risk, and is never given prophylactically. Would be correct only if the infant developed clinical haemorrhage with deranged coagulation despite vitamin K (Royal Women's Hospital neonatal guidelines).

rationales.A: Continuing the same ICS dose is wrong because deteriorating control (reliever more than twice weekly, nocturnal symptoms, falling FEV1) defines uncontrolled asthma, which is more dangerous in pregnancy than dose escalation; the anti-pattern is therapeutic inertia in the second trimester. Would be correct only if control were stable and symptoms minimal, which is not the case here (RANZCOG; TSANZ; Australian Asthma Handbook).
rationales.C: Stopping ICS is wrong because pregnancy is the exact setting where the Australian Asthma Handbook insists ICS be continued; cessation is associated with exacerbations, pre-eclampsia, prematurity and low birth weight. Would never be correct as a planned change in pregnancy (TSANZ; Australian Asthma Handbook).
rationales.D: Oral prednisolone as maintenance is wrong because oral corticosteroids are reserved for exacerbations, not chronic baseline therapy, due to maternal glycaemic, hypertensive and bone effects; the step-up ladder goes through inhaled ICS-LABA first. Would be correct only if she had severe steroid-dependent asthma already on optimised inhaled therapy (TSANZ; Australian Asthma Handbook).
rationales.E: Montelukast monotherapy is wrong because leukotriene receptor antagonists are an add-on, not a substitute for ICS, in the GINA/TSANZ pathway. Would be correct as a continuation if she was already on montelukast preconception with good control, not as a first-line switch (TSANZ; Australian Asthma Handbook).

rationales.A: Oral montelukast loading is wrong because LTRAs have no acute bronchodilator effect and onset is hours to days; they are chronic add-on agents, not rescue therapy. Would be correct only as part of chronic step-up after discharge, not in acute severe asthma (National Asthma Council Australia).
rationales.B: IV aminophylline is wrong because of its narrow therapeutic window, arrhythmogenic risk and lack of mortality benefit over magnesium; current Australian practice reserves it for refractory cases after IV magnesium has failed. Would be correct only when IV magnesium has been given and the patient is still deteriorating in ICU, not as the next step (Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory).
rationales.C: IM adrenaline is wrong because it is anaphylaxis therapy, and in pregnancy uteroplacental vasoconstriction is a specific concern; the anti-pattern is reaching for adrenaline when the diagnosis is severe asthma not anaphylaxis. Would be correct only if anaphylaxis were the actual mechanism (ASCIA; National Asthma Council Australia).
rationales.D: Emergency caesarean is wrong because delivery is not a treatment for maternal asthma and the foetus is currently reassured; surgery and intubation would worsen bronchospasm. Would be correct only if maternal arrest were imminent or peri-mortem caesarean criteria were met, not at this stage (RANZCOG; Royal Women's Hospital).

rationales.A: Maintaining 100 mcg is wrong because thyroid hormone requirements rise 25-50% in the first trimester driven by hCG-stimulated TBG; delay risks maternal hypothyroidism with neurodevelopmental impact on the foetus, and the Endocrine Society recommends an empirical bump on confirmation of pregnancy. Would be correct only if pre-pregnancy TSH was already optimised at the lower end and a recheck-only strategy was agreed, which is not standard (RANZCOG; Endocrine Society).
rationales.B: Stopping iodine is wrong because NHMRC recommends 150 mcg iodine for all Australian pregnant and lactating women regardless of thyroxine status; iodine supports placental and foetal thyroid synthesis, while levothyroxine replaces maternal hormone. Would never be correct in an Australian antenatal context (NHMRC).
rationales.C: Doubling to 200 mcg is wrong because the evidence-based empirical increment is 20-30% (typically two extra tablets per week); doubling risks iatrogenic thyrotoxicosis with atrial fibrillation and growth restriction. Would be correct only in newly diagnosed overt hypothyroidism with very high TSH at booking, not for stable preconception disease (Endocrine Society).
rationales.D: Switching to liothyronine is wrong because T3 crosses the placenta poorly and the foetus depends on maternal T4 for cerebral development; combination or T3-only therapy is explicitly discouraged in pregnancy. Would be correct only in extremely rare specialist scenarios, never in routine antenatal care (RANZCOG; Endocrine Society).

rationales.A: Continuing carbimazole in the first trimester is wrong because it is associated with embryopathy (aplasia cutis, choanal and oesophageal atresia, omphalocoele) during organogenesis; the standard carve-out is PTU until 16 weeks then switch back. Would be correct only after the first trimester is complete, not from 6 weeks (RANZCOG; Endocrine Society).
rationales.B: Radioactive iodine ablation is wrong because it is an absolute contraindication in pregnancy due to foetal thyroid ablation and teratogenic radiation exposure. Would be correct only postpartum after cessation of breastfeeding with appropriate isolation precautions (Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology).
rationales.C: Stopping all antithyroid drug is wrong because biochemically active Graves disease with raised TRAb risks maternal thyroid storm, foetal goitre and foetal tachycardia; the anti-pattern is treating pregnancy itself as remission. Would be correct only if she were in true biochemical remission with negative TRAb and stable off therapy preconception (Endocrine Society).
rationales.E: Adding propranolol while continuing carbimazole is wrong because beta blockade addresses adrenergic symptoms (which she does not have) without removing the teratogenic exposure during organogenesis. Would be appropriate as a short-term adjunct in symptomatic thyrotoxicosis or thyroid storm, not as a strategy to neutralise carbimazole risk (Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology).

rationales.B: Stopping hydroxychloroquine is wrong because HCQ is the one drug we deliberately keep going through pregnancy in SLE: it reduces flares, reduces recurrence of congenital heart block in subsequent pregnancies, and may reduce pre-eclampsia. Would be correct only if she developed retinopathy or another HCQ-specific adverse effect, not because of the pregnancy itself (EULAR SLE pregnancy guidelines; RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Empirical IVIG is wrong because trials have failed to show that IVIG prevents first-occurrence congenital heart block; the established strategy is foetal echo surveillance with prompt intervention if first-degree block emerges. Would be considered only as research therapy or in selected high-risk recurrence cases at specialist discretion, not as routine primary prevention (EULAR).
rationales.D: Stopping aspirin is wrong because SLE carries a high pre-eclampsia risk and SOMANZ recommends low-dose aspirin from before 16 weeks for this exact indication; anti-Ro status does not change bleeding risk meaningfully. Would be correct only with active bleeding or genuine aspirin allergy (SOMANZ; RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Empirical LMWH is wrong because thromboprophylaxis is reserved for antiphospholipid syndrome, prior VTE or otherwise high VTE risk; SLE alone with negative APL antibodies does not meet RCOG thromboprophylaxis criteria. Would be correct in coexisting APS, not in isolated SLE (RCOG; RANZCOG).

rationales.A: Therapeutic-dose enoxaparin alone is wrong because the obstetric APS regimen pairs prophylactic (not therapeutic) LMWH with aspirin; therapeutic dose is reserved for APS with prior thrombosis or current VTE. Would be correct only if she had previous thrombotic APS, not pure obstetric APS (RCOG Green-top 17; RANZCOG).
rationales.B: Aspirin alone is wrong because aspirin monotherapy has not consistently reduced miscarriage in obstetric APS; the synergistic effect comes from aspirin plus LMWH. Would be correct only for women with isolated APL antibodies and no obstetric APS criteria met, not for true obstetric APS like this case (RCOG Green-top 17).
rationales.C: Warfarin titration is wrong because warfarin is teratogenic from approximately 6 to 12 weeks (warfarin embryopathy with nasal hypoplasia and chondrodysplasia punctata); LMWH is the pregnancy anticoagulant of choice. Would be correct only in mechanical heart valve scenarios with explicit risk-benefit counselling, not in obstetric APS (Therapeutic Guidelines; RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Rivaroxaban is wrong because DOACs cross the placenta, lack safety data and are explicitly contraindicated in pregnancy; the TRAPS trial also showed rivaroxaban inferiority to warfarin in triple-positive APS outside pregnancy. Would never be correct in pregnant obstetric APS (Therapeutic Guidelines; AMH).

rationales.A: Routine elective caesarean is wrong because the ASHM/BHIVA threshold for offering caesarean is viral load at or above 50 copies/mL (or unknown) near term; with sustained suppression, vaginal birth has equivalent transmission risk and lower maternal morbidity. Would be correct only with detectable or undocumented viral load close to delivery (ASHM; BHIVA).
rationales.C: Stopping ART is wrong because viral rebound after cessation is rapid and would directly increase vertical transmission risk in late pregnancy and labour; the anti-pattern is treating ART as optional once viral load is suppressed. Would never be correct in pregnancy (ASHM).
rationales.D: Elective caesarean plus IV zidovudine is wrong because intrapartum IV zidovudine is reserved for viral load at or above 400 copies/mL or unknown status; layering caesarean plus IV zidovudine on a virologically suppressed woman adds morbidity without benefit. Would be correct only if her viral load were not suppressed (BHIVA).
rationales.E: Induction at 37 weeks is wrong because there is no evidence early induction reduces vertical transmission in suppressed women; obstetric indications drive timing of birth, and iatrogenic prematurity carries neonatal morbidity. Would be correct only for standard obstetric reasons, not as HIV-specific intervention (ASHM; RANZCOG).

rationales.A: Triple infant ART is wrong because high-risk neonatal regimens (zidovudine plus lamivudine plus nevirapine or raltegravir) are reserved for infants of mothers with detectable VL near term, late diagnosis or seroconversion in pregnancy; this infant is low risk. Would be correct only if maternal viral load were not suppressed at delivery (BHIVA).
rationales.B: Exclusive breastfeeding is wrong because Australian ASHM guidance recommends formula feeding in high resource settings: residual transmission risk is not zero even on ART, and formula is safe and accessible. Would be the WHO recommendation in resource-limited settings where replacement feeding is not AFASS, not in Australia (ASHM; WHO).
rationales.D: Mixed feeding is wrong because it is the highest-risk feeding pattern: HIV exposure from breast milk combined with mucosal disruption from formula. Would never be correct for HIV-exposed infants (WHO; ASHM).
rationales.E: No infant prophylaxis is wrong because all HIV-exposed infants receive at least short-course zidovudine even with suppressed maternal VL to cover residual transmission risk. Would be correct only if HIV status had been completely excluded antenatally, not after confirmed maternal HIV (ASHM; RANZCOG).

rationales.A: Maternal HBIG is wrong because passive immunoglobulin given to the mother does not reduce viral load and HBIG has no role in maternal antenatal prevention; HBIG belongs to the infant within 12 hours of birth. Would be correct only for non-immune individuals after high-risk exposure, not as antenatal prophylaxis (Australian Immunisation Handbook).
rationales.B: Entecavir is wrong because Australian guidance prefers tenofovir disoproxil in pregnancy due to a much larger safety dataset; entecavir is Pregnancy Category B3 with limited human data. Would be correct only in tenofovir intolerance or resistance, not first-line (ASHM; GESA).
rationales.D: Elective caesarean is wrong because mode of delivery does not change HBV transmission when infant HBIG plus birth-dose vaccine are given; the anti-pattern is using caesarean as a viral barrier the way it works for HIV. Would be appropriate only for standard obstetric indications (ASHM; RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Advising against breastfeeding is wrong because HBIG plus birth-dose vaccine makes breastfeeding safe even with high maternal DNA; the Australian Immunisation Handbook explicitly endorses breastfeeding in HBV-positive women. Would be correct only with cracked nipples and uncovered infant (Australian Immunisation Handbook; ASHM).

rationales.B: Starting DAA at 12 weeks is wrong because sofosbuvir/velpatasvir is not yet recommended outside research protocols in Australian pregnancy due to limited pharmacokinetic and safety data; deferring to postpartum is standard. Would be correct only within a registered trial, not as routine care (ASHM; GESA).
rationales.C: Elective caesarean is wrong because HCV vertical transmission is not reduced by caesarean delivery; intrapartum factors (prolonged ROM, foetal scalp electrodes) matter more than mode of birth. Would be appropriate only for standard obstetric indications (ASHM; RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Advising against breastfeeding is wrong because HCV is not transmitted through intact lactation; ASHM and ABA explicitly recommend breastfeeding with the carve-out to pause feeding from a side with cracked or bleeding nipples. Would be correct only with active nipple trauma on the involved side (ASHM; Australian Breastfeeding Association).
rationales.E: Neonatal anti-HCV at 48 hours is wrong because passively acquired maternal antibody persists in the infant for up to 18 months, making early antibody testing uninformative; HCV PCR at 2 and 6 months or antibody after 18 months is the recommended schedule. Would be correct only for documenting transient maternal antibody, not for diagnosing infant infection (ASHM).

rationales.A: Doing nothing is wrong because >50 x10^9/L is the threshold for vaginal birth without instrumentation, not for neuraxial anaesthesia, which requires >70-80 x10^9/L per ASH 2019 and Australian practice. Would be correct only if she planned vaginal birth without epidural and no operative interventions (ASH 2019; KEMH).
rationales.C: Pre-emptive platelet transfusion is wrong because platelet survival is short in ITP due to antibody-mediated destruction, so transfusion does not provide durable counts; it is reserved for active bleeding or imminent surgery. Would be correct only at the time of caesarean or with bleeding, not as antenatal optimisation (ASH 2019).
rationales.D: Eltrombopag is wrong because TPO receptor agonists are second/third-line in ITP and have limited pregnancy safety data; first-line in pregnancy is prednisolone with IVIG as second-line. Would be correct only in refractory ITP after corticosteroids and IVIG have failed (Therapeutic Guidelines; ASH 2019).
rationales.E: Discussing splenectomy is wrong because the question asks about delivery-time platelet optimisation and splenectomy does not address the current pregnancy; it is also factually misleading - splenectomy remains an option for selected refractory chronic ITP. Would be a postpartum discussion only after standard therapies fail (ASH 2019).

rationales.A: Stopping azathioprine and switching to budesonide is wrong because thiopurines maintain remission whereas budesonide is induction therapy with limited long-term maintenance efficacy; flares in pregnancy harm the foetus more than the drug. Would be correct only if she were intolerant of azathioprine and seeking short-term flare control, not as preconception maintenance (GESA; ECCO).
rationales.C: Stopping adalimumab at conception is wrong because anti-TNF agents are continued at least through to 24-30 weeks (often to delivery, depending on the specific biologic) because flare risk outweighs theoretical immunogenicity; the anti-pattern is reflexive drug holidays. Would be correct only if she were in deep remission with biomarker confirmation and accepted flare risk, and even then most guidelines do not endorse cessation (GESA; ECCO).
rationales.D: Restarting methotrexate is wrong because methotrexate is teratogenic (folate antagonism, NTDs, craniofacial anomalies) and is contraindicated through pregnancy and the 3-6 month preconception window for both partners. Would never be correct during conception attempts or pregnancy (Therapeutic Guidelines: Rheumatology).
rationales.E: Switching to mycophenolate is wrong because MMF is teratogenic (EMFO syndrome - cleft lip/palate, microtia, cardiac, oesophageal) and explicitly contraindicated in pregnancy; pregnancy prevention is required during MMF use. Would be correct only outside pregnancy planning, never as preconception maintenance (Therapeutic Guidelines: Rheumatology).

rationales.A: Substituting metformin for insulin is wrong because metformin does not provide adequate glycaemic control in type 1 diabetes and does not address the absolute insulin deficiency; pre-existing T1DM requires intensive insulin therapy. Would be appropriate only as an adjunct in type 2 diabetes or PCOS, not as substitution in T1DM (ADIPS).
rationales.B: Reassuring about HbA1c 8.4% is wrong because this level corresponds to substantially elevated rates of major congenital anomaly, miscarriage and stillbirth; ADIPS targets <6.5% (or <7.0% if hypoglycaemia limits) before conception attempts. Would be correct only if no further optimisation were achievable and she had been counselled on residual risk, which is not the case here (ADIPS).
rationales.D: Advising against pregnancy is wrong because background retinopathy is not a contraindication; pregnancy is permitted with optimised glycaemia and antenatal ophthalmology surveillance every trimester. Would be correct only for severe untreated proliferative retinopathy or advanced cardiac/renal complications (ADIPS; RANZCOG).
rationales.E: SGLT2 inhibitors are wrong because they precipitate euglycaemic DKA in T1DM, are contraindicated in pregnancy and have no role in preconception glycaemic intensification. Would never be correct in T1DM preconception planning (Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology).

rationales.A: Continuing the pump with 4-hourly checks is wrong because intrapartum T1DM needs hourly capillary glucose to stay in the narrow 4-7 mmol/L band; pumps may continue only with strict hourly monitoring and a written failure protocol. Would be correct only with hourly testing and explicit pump-continuation protocol, not 4-hourly (ADIPS; RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Stopping all insulin is wrong because basal insulin withdrawal in T1DM precipitates DKA within hours; the anti-pattern is treating T1DM like T2DM where insulin can be paused. Would never be correct in T1DM intrapartum (Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology).
rationales.D: Higher targets are wrong because intrapartum maternal hyperglycaemia drives foetal hyperinsulinism and neonatal hypoglycaemia after cord clamp; 4-7 mmol/L is the established target. Would be correct only as a transient ceiling during hypoglycaemia recovery, not as a deliberate target (ADIPS).
rationales.E: Single long-acting glargine bolus is wrong because long-acting analogues are not titratable and cannot match the rapid changes in insulin requirement during labour and immediately postpartum; variable rate IV insulin is preferred. Would be correct only as an outpatient regimen, not in active labour (ADIPS).

rationales.A: LMWH throughout is wrong because mechanical mitral valve thrombosis rates are higher on LMWH-only regimens than on warfarin-containing strategies despite anti-Xa monitoring; ESC reserves this approach for women who decline warfarin after structured counselling. Would be correct only as a patient-preference option after counselling, not as default (ESC 2018).
rationales.B: Warfarin throughout is wrong because it carries warfarin embryopathy risk between 6 and 12 weeks (nasal hypoplasia, chondrodysplasia punctata) and foetal intracranial haemorrhage at delivery; pure-warfarin strategy is acceptable only when daily warfarin dose is low (<5 mg) with explicit counselling. Would be correct only when warfarin dose is <5 mg/day and the woman accepts foetal risk after counselling, not as default for high-dose warfarin requirements (ESC 2018).
rationales.D: Rivaroxaban is wrong because DOACs are contraindicated in pregnancy and ineffective at preventing mechanical valve thrombosis (RE-ALIGN trial showed harm with dabigatran). Would never be correct in mechanical valves, pregnant or not (ESC 2018; AMH).
rationales.E: Stopping anticoagulation in the first trimester is wrong because mechanical mitral valves have very high thrombotic risk and require uninterrupted anticoagulation; periods without anticoagulant cause valve thrombosis and maternal death. Would never be correct (ESC 2018).

rationales.A: Apixaban is wrong because DOACs are not recommended for postpartum thromboprophylaxis in Australia, partly due to limited lactation data and partly because LMWH has stronger evidence; the anti-pattern is extrapolating non-pregnant orthopaedic practice. Would be correct only outside the postpartum/breastfeeding context (RANZCOG; Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.B: Mechanical prophylaxis alone is wrong because emergency caesarean plus multiple risk factors crosses the threshold for pharmacological prophylaxis; stockings are an adjunct, not a substitute. Would be correct only when LMWH is contraindicated (e.g. active bleeding) (RCOG Green-top 37a).
rationales.C: Therapeutic-dose LMWH is wrong because therapeutic dosing is for confirmed VTE or very-high-risk prophylaxis (recent VTE, APS with prior thrombosis); the bleeding risk is unjustified for prophylactic indications. Would be correct only if she had had a recent VTE or thrombotic APS (RCOG Green-top 37a).
rationales.E: Aspirin is wrong because aspirin lacks evidence for postnatal VTE prevention in this risk profile and is not recommended by RCOG Green-top 37a; LMWH is the agent of choice. Would be correct only as pre-eclampsia prophylaxis, not VTE (RCOG Green-top 37a).

rationales.B: Emollients alone with 39-40 week delivery is wrong because emollients are purely symptomatic and do not address the underlying cholestasis; the stillbirth curve at bile acids 56 micromol/L rises substantially after 38 weeks, making term expectancy unsafe. Would be correct only for mild ICP (bile acids <40 micromol/L) where 39-40 week delivery is acceptable (RCOG ICP 2022).
rationales.C: Cholestyramine plus immediate delivery is wrong because cholestyramine has fallen out of first-line use due to fat-soluble vitamin malabsorption (including vitamin K) and immediate delivery at 35+2 is reserved for severe disease at bile acids >=100 micromol/L. Would be correct only at severe-disease thresholds, not at 56 micromol/L (RCOG ICP 2022; RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Dexamethasone is wrong because corticosteroids are not effective primary treatment for ICP; UDCA is the established pharmacological agent. Would be correct only for antenatal lung maturation if early preterm delivery were planned, not as ICP therapy (RCOG ICP 2022).
rationales.E: Antihistamines plus 40-week expectancy is wrong because chlorphenamine only modestly improves itch and does not change bile acid kinetics or stillbirth risk; delivery to 40 weeks at this bile acid level exceeds the safety window. Would be correct only for benign pruritus of pregnancy without raised bile acids (RCOG ICP 2022).

rationales.A: Hypertension and proteinuria is wrong because it is more characteristic of HELLP (which sits on the pre-eclampsia spectrum) than AFLP, where blood pressure is often only modestly elevated and proteinuria less prominent. Would point to HELLP/pre-eclampsia rather than AFLP (RANZCOG).
rationales.B: RUQ pain with transaminases >500 is wrong because both AFLP and HELLP can present with this picture; it is shared rather than discriminating. Would be neutral, not diagnostic, between the two (Swansea criteria).
rationales.C: Platelets <100 is wrong because it is part of HELLP criteria and can occur in AFLP from synthetic failure too; in AFLP the platelet drop is usually modest relative to the coagulopathy. Would be a HELLP criterion not a discriminator (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Hyperuricaemia is wrong because raised urate is a classic pre-eclampsia/HELLP marker (placental ischaemia, reduced renal clearance) and is not specific to AFLP. Would point to HELLP/pre-eclampsia (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: MCDA labelling is wrong because the lambda sign is the dichorionic marker; the T-sign (membrane meeting placenta at 90 degrees with no chorion wedge) defines MCDA. Would be correct only if the scan showed a T-sign, not a lambda (RANZCOG twins; ISUOG).
rationales.C: MCMA labelling is wrong because monoamniotic twinning requires a single sac with no inter-twin membrane; the report describes two sacs and a thick separating membrane. Would be correct only with no visible membrane and twins moving in a shared sac (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Saying chorionicity cannot be assigned until 11-14 weeks is wrong because first-trimester assessment is most accurate and the lambda/T sign is reliable from around 7-9 weeks; deferring assessment loses the most accurate window. Would be correct only if first-trimester views are technically inadequate, requiring rescan (ISUOG).
rationales.E: Dichorionic monoamniotic is wrong because it is not a real entity: each chorion brings its own amnion, so two chorions means two amnions. Would never be a valid classification (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: Reassurance and fortnightly surveillance is wrong because untreated Quintero stage II TTTS has high perinatal mortality; the anti-pattern is waiting for progression. Would be correct only for Stage I with stable Dopplers under close specialist review, not Stage II (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Amnioreduction is wrong because the Eurofoetus and subsequent trials showed fetoscopic laser ablation gives superior dual-survival and lower neurological morbidity than amnioreduction in stage II-IV TTTS at 16-26 weeks. Would be acceptable only if laser is unavailable, the woman declines transfer, or gestation is outside the laser window (RANZCOG; ISUOG).
rationales.D: Antenatal corticosteroids and delivery at 19+5 is wrong because 19+5 weeks is pre-viable; delivery would result in dual neonatal loss. Would be correct only at periviable gestations after 24 weeks with stage III-IV TTTS refractory to laser (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Cord occlusion of the donor is wrong because selective reduction by cord occlusion is reserved for Stage IV-V or when laser has failed/is not feasible (e.g. severe hydrops in one twin, anterior placenta with poor access); at Stage II laser is first line. Would be correct only as salvage therapy when laser is unsuitable (RANZCOG; ISUOG).

rationales.A: Type I labelling is wrong because Gratacos Type I has persistently positive UA end-diastolic flow with a favourable prognosis; intermittent absent end-diastolic flow defines Type III. Would be correct only if UA Dopplers remained continuously positive (Gratacos sIUGR classification).
rationales.B: Type II labelling is wrong because Type II shows persistently absent or reversed UA flow with progressive deterioration and earlier delivery (often 28-30 weeks); the intermittent pattern here is Type III. Would be correct only if AEDF or REDF were persistent across studies (Gratacos sIUGR classification).
rationales.D: Reassuring as benign discordance is wrong because EFW below the 10th centile in either twin combined with greater than 25% discordance meets the consensus definition of sIUGR; the anti-pattern is treating this as physiological variation. Would be correct only if both twins were AGA with milder discordance, not here (ISUOG).
rationales.E: TTTS labelling is wrong because TTTS requires discordant fluid (DVP <2 cm in one and >8 cm in the other); fluid is normal in both sacs, so the pathology is sIUGR not TTTS. Would be correct only if oligohydramnios-polyhydramnios were present (RANZCOG; ISUOG).

rationales.B: Anti-D 250 IU is wrong because the NBA dose increases to 625 IU IM from 13+0 weeks for sensitising events; 250 IU is the pre-12-week dose. Would be correct only before 13 weeks, not at exactly 13+0 (National Blood Authority Anti-D guidelines).
rationales.C: Withholding anti-D until 28 weeks is wrong because sensitising events require anti-D within 72 hours regardless of negative antibody screen or upcoming routine prophylaxis; waiting risks sensitisation. Would be correct only for a non-sensitising event with no bleeding (National Blood Authority).
rationales.D: Surgical evacuation is wrong because vanishing twins resorb naturally and instrumentation risks loss of the surviving twin from membrane disruption or infection. Would be correct only if there were septic retained products threatening the surviving pregnancy, which is not described (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Serial coagulation studies for DIC is wrong because maternal DIC risk applies to single-twin demise after viability (typically >=20 weeks) with prolonged in-utero retention; first-trimester vanishing twin does not generate the necessary thromboplastin load. Would be correct only for second/third-trimester co-twin demise (RANZCOG).

rationales.A: 38-39 weeks is wrong because MCDA twins are not safely carried that far: shared circulation drives a stillbirth curve that rises sharply after 36+6, and RANZCOG sets the planned-birth window at 36+0 to 36+6. Would be correct only for uncomplicated DCDA twins (RANZCOG twins; KEMH).
rationales.C: Elective caesarean at 36+0 is wrong because chorionicity alone does not mandate caesarean; with twin 1 cephalic and no other contraindications, planned vaginal birth is recommended. Would be correct only if twin 1 were non-cephalic or another caesarean indication existed (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Caesarean at 32+0 is wrong because iatrogenic delivery at 32 weeks imposes major prematurity morbidity (RDS, NEC, IVH) without benefit in an uncomplicated MCDA pregnancy. Would be correct only with severe TTTS/sIUGR or significant maternal-foetal compromise (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: 37+0 caesarean regardless of presentation is wrong on two counts: 37+0 sits beyond the RANZCOG MCDA window and caesarean is not mandated when twin 1 is cephalic. Would be correct only if both twins were past the window and twin 1 was non-cephalic (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: 36+0 to 36+6 is wrong because that is the MCDA window; in MCMA, cord entanglement risk rises after 32 weeks and waiting to 36 is unsafe. Would be correct for MCDA, not MCMA (RANZCOG; KEMH).
rationales.C: Vaginal birth with CTG is wrong because cord entanglement in a shared sac makes labour intrinsically high-risk regardless of presentation; the anti-pattern is treating MCMA like MCDA. Would never be correct in MCMA, even with cephalic-cephalic presentation (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Aiming for 37+0 vaginally is wrong because it stacks the worst of both errors: extending well past the MCMA safety window plus exposing twins to labour-related entanglement risk. Would never be correct in MCMA (RANZCOG; KEMH).
rationales.E: Immediate caesarean at 31+4 is wrong because without a deterioration trigger (abnormal CTG, sIUGR progression, TTTS), birth at 31+4 imposes prematurity morbidity that the 32-34 week window is designed to avoid. Would be correct only with evidence of foetal compromise or maternal indication (RANZCOG).

rationales.A: Nifedipine is wrong because Therapeutic Guidelines flag a relative contraindication when co-prescribed with antihypertensives like labetalol due to additive hypotension and risk of uteroplacental compromise. Would be correct only in women not on antihypertensives without cardiovascular concerns (Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetrics).
rationales.C: Indomethacin is wrong because it is generally avoided from around 32 weeks due to foetal ductus arteriosus constriction and oligohydramnios; even though 28+3 is technically within window, atosiban has a better maternal safety profile here. Would be a reasonable alternative under 32 weeks if atosiban is unavailable (Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetrics).
rationales.D: Magnesium sulfate as tocolysis is wrong because magnesium has been shown ineffective as a tocolytic and is instead reserved for foetal neuroprotection at <30 weeks. Would be correct only as neuroprotection (the question separately requires this), not tocolysis (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: IV salbutamol is wrong because beta-agonists are no longer recommended in Australia for tocolysis (maternal tachycardia, pulmonary oedema, hyperglycaemia, hypokalaemia); they have been withdrawn from first-line practice. Would never be first-line in current Australian guidance (Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetrics).

rationales.B: Pulmonary oedema is wrong because that adverse profile belongs to beta-agonists (and prolonged combination tocolysis), not indomethacin; the question targets indomethacin-specific harm at 33+1. Would point to salbutamol or atosiban concerns instead (Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetrics).
rationales.C: Maternal hypotension is wrong because vasodilator hypotension is a calcium channel blocker (nifedipine) effect, not an indomethacin effect; indomethacin acts on prostaglandin synthesis. Would describe nifedipine, not indomethacin (Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetrics).
rationales.D: Prior preterm birth as absolute contraindication is wrong because prior preterm birth is the indication for treatment, not a contraindication; gestational age and renal/GI status are the relevant flags. Would never be a valid contraindication (Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetrics).
rationales.E: Reduced steroid efficacy is wrong because there is no evidence indomethacin attenuates betamethasone lung maturation effect; the late-gestation concern is foetal ductus and oligohydramnios. Would not be a recognised concern (Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetrics).

rationales.A: Giving betamethasone at 36+2 is wrong because RANZCOG limits routine antenatal corticosteroids to 24+0 to 34+6 weeks; routine use beyond 35 weeks does not improve neonatal respiratory outcomes enough to outweigh harms including neonatal hypoglycaemia. Would be correct only within 24+0 to 34+6 with imminent birth (RANZCOG ANC guideline).
rationales.C: Dexamethasone 6 mg q12h x4 is wrong because the RANZCOG dexamethasone regimen is 12 mg IM 24 hours apart for two doses; the option misstates the dosing and gives steroids outside the gestational window. Would never match Australian dosing (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Giving within an hour of birth is wrong because corticosteroids require several hours to upregulate surfactant production; the gestational cutoff is the issue here, not timing within the same hour. Would be a misconception about mechanism (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Rescue dose at 36+2 is wrong because rescue courses are considered before 34+0 weeks when the prior course was over 7 days ago and birth is imminent; 36+2 is outside the window. Would be correct only at <34+0 with prior course >7 days earlier (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: Full repeat course is wrong because Australian guidance favours a single rescue dose rather than a full repeat; multiple repeat courses are associated with reduced birth weight and head circumference. Would be correct only in some international protocols, not RANZCOG (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Withholding steroids entirely is wrong because evidence (ACTORDS, MACS analyses) shows a rescue dose provides meaningful neonatal benefit when the prior course was over 7 days ago and birth is now imminent. Would be correct only if the original course were within the past 7 days (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Dexamethasone 6 mg single dose is wrong because Australian dexamethasone dose is 12 mg per dose, and the preferred rescue agent is betamethasone 11.4 mg IM; the dose stated is half-strength. Would never match Australian rescue dosing (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Withholding until >5 cm is wrong because steroid foetal benefit takes hours to develop; the anti-pattern is treating steroid timing as a labour-progress decision rather than an anticipation-of-birth decision. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: Saying MgSO4 is only for pre-eclampsia is wrong because the foetal neuroprotection role is established independently (ACTOMgSO4, Cochrane meta-analysis NNT ~50 to prevent CP at very preterm gestations). Would be correct only if neuroprotection benefit had not been demonstrated, which is now well established (RANZCOG; SOMANZ).
rationales.C: Single IM dose is wrong because the regimen for neuroprotection is IV loading (4 g over 20-30 min) then maintenance infusion (1 g/hr); IM dosing does not achieve the required serum levels. Would never be the correct route (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Withholding magnesium because steroids were given is wrong because corticosteroids (lung maturation) and magnesium (neuroprotection) act on different pathways and have independent benefit; the anti-pattern is treating them as interchangeable. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Deferring at 29+4 is wrong because Australian guidance offers neuroprotection up to <30 weeks (with some centres extending to <32 weeks); 29+4 sits inside the window. Would be correct only at gestations beyond local cutoffs (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: Co-amoxiclav is wrong because the ORACLE I trial demonstrated increased neonatal necrotising enterocolitis with co-amoxiclav in PPROM; it is explicitly avoided. Would be correct only outside PPROM (RANZCOG; ORACLE I).
rationales.C: Benzylpenicillin alone for 48h is wrong because it is intrapartum GBS prophylaxis, not a latency regimen, and does not provide the prolongation seen with macrolides; the anti-pattern is conflating GBS prophylaxis with PPROM latency therapy. Would be correct only as intrapartum GBS cover when in labour (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Single-dose azithromycin is wrong because that regimen is used as adjunct prophylaxis at caesarean (CSection trial), not as PPROM latency cover; PPROM requires a 10-day macrolide course. Would be correct only as caesarean prophylaxis (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Withholding antibiotics until chorioamnionitis is wrong because PPROM <34 weeks routinely receives latency antibiotics; the evidence base predates clinical infection. Would be correct only at term PROM, not preterm (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: Outpatient expectant management is wrong because near-term PPROM with GBS positivity carries clear risk of chorioamnionitis and early-onset neonatal GBS sepsis, and outpatient observation prevents timely intrapartum penicillin. Would be correct only if she were GBS-negative and could tolerate close inpatient observation, not at home with GBS positivity (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Tocolysis plus steroids is wrong because steroids are not routinely given beyond 35 weeks and tocolysis only prolongs membrane rupture while increasing infection; the anti-pattern is treating 36+5 like a much earlier preterm scenario. Would never be correct at this gestation (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Caesarean for PPROM at this gestation is wrong because PPROM is not a caesarean indication; vaginal birth after induction is the preferred route in the absence of other contraindications. Would be correct only with obstetric indications for caesarean (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Inpatient expectancy to 39+0 is wrong because prolonging ruptured membranes with GBS positivity drives chorioamnionitis and neonatal sepsis; the TERMPROM trial and RANZCOG support expediting birth. Would be correct only with reassuring clinical picture and GBS negativity, perhaps to 37 weeks, not 39 (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: Vaginal progesterone alone is wrong because progesterone is indicated for asymptomatic short cervix on TVS or singleton prior preterm birth, not as a substitute for cerclage in three prior second-trimester losses. Would be correct only as adjunct, not as sole strategy in this history (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Serial scans with rescue cerclage is wrong because women with three prior mid-trimester losses meet criteria for history-indicated (elective) cerclage; ultrasound-indicated cerclage is the strategy for women with one prior preterm birth where the cervix may not shorten. Would be correct only with one prior preterm birth, not three losses (RANZCOG; RCOG cerclage guideline).
rationales.D: Preconception transabdominal cerclage is wrong because TAC is reserved for failed transvaginal cerclage or anatomical features (very short cervix, extensive trachelectomy) precluding vaginal placement; not first-line. Would be correct after a failed vaginal cerclage in a previous pregnancy (RANZCOG; RCOG).
rationales.E: Cervical pessary is wrong because the evidence is mixed (PECEP positive, ProTWIN/OPPTIMUM neutral) and pessary is not first-line in Australian practice for this history. Would be correct only in research settings or specific resource-limited contexts (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: IM 17OHPC is wrong because the PROLONG trial confirmed lack of efficacy and the FDA withdrew approval; vaginal progesterone has the supporting evidence (PREGNANT, OPPTIMUM, individual patient meta-analyses). Would never be correct in current Australian practice (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Rescue cerclage is wrong because rescue cerclage is for advanced cervical dilation with exposed membranes; this woman has a closed short cervix. Would be correct only with dilated cervix and bulging membranes (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Bedrest is wrong because trials have shown bedrest does not reduce preterm birth and increases VTE and muscle deconditioning; the anti-pattern is offering bedrest as a 'do something' intervention. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: qfFN testing is wrong because fFN has its strongest predictive role in symptomatic women; in an asymptomatic woman with short cervix the management trigger (progesterone) is already in place regardless of fFN. Would be correct only in symptomatic threatened preterm labour (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: Admit, steroids and tocolysis is wrong because qfFN <50 ng/mL with a closed cervix has very high NPV for birth within 7 days; admission and steroid courses are unwarranted. Would be correct only with positive qfFN or cervical change (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Magnesium sulfate is wrong because neuroprotective MgSO4 requires birth anticipated within 24 hours, which the negative fFN explicitly refutes; the anti-pattern is using MgSO4 'just in case'. Would be correct only with imminent birth at <30 weeks (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Repeat fFN in 24h is wrong because management does not change for a low qfFN value with no cervical change; the anti-pattern is using repeat testing as a substitute for clinical decision-making. Would be correct only if the clinical picture changed (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Emergency rescue cerclage is wrong because cerclage requires advanced cervical dilation with bulging membranes, not a closed cervix with negative fFN. Would be correct only with cervical dilation and exposed membranes (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: Elective IOL at 37+0 is wrong because routine 37+0 induction in well-controlled diet-only GDM increases iatrogenic late preterm respiratory morbidity without improving stillbirth or shoulder dystocia outcomes; ADIPS recommends ~40+6. Would be correct only with insulin-treated GDM or comorbidities (ADIPS; RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Elective caesarean at 38+0 is wrong because diet-controlled GDM does not in itself indicate caesarean; macrosomia thresholds and obstetric indications drive mode of birth. Would be correct only with EFW >4500 g, prior caesarean or other indication (ADIPS; RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Induction at 39+0 regardless of Bishop is wrong because diet-controlled uncomplicated GDM can be managed expectantly to around 40+6 per ADIPS; routine early induction is reserved for insulin-treated or complicated disease. Would be correct only for insulin-treated GDM or other complications (ADIPS; RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Deferring to 41+5 is wrong because even diet-controlled GDM raises stillbirth risk beyond 41 weeks; the upper boundary in ADIPS is around 40+6. Would never be correct in GDM regardless of control (ADIPS).

rationales.B: Expectancy with no antibiotics unless febrile is wrong because GBS-positive PROM warrants IAP from the time of presentation; waiting for fever signals fully established chorioamnionitis. Would be correct only in GBS-negative term PROM with stable observations (RANZCOG GBS).
rationales.C: Home expectancy 5 days is wrong because outpatient management beyond 24h in term PROM raises chorioamnionitis risk and is incompatible with timely IV penicillin in a GBS-positive woman. Would never be correct in GBS-positive PROM (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Mandatory caesarean is wrong because vaginal birth with IAP is the standard approach in GBS-positive PROM; caesarean does not eliminate vertical transmission and adds maternal morbidity. Would be correct only for standard obstetric indications (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Erythromycin for latency is wrong because erythromycin latency therapy is for preterm PROM; at term, the goal is induction, not prolongation. Would be correct only at <34 weeks PPROM (RANZCOG; ORACLE I).

rationales.B: Continued expectancy to 32 weeks is wrong because once clinical chorioamnionitis is established, prolonging pregnancy drives maternal sepsis and foetal injury (white matter injury, neonatal sepsis); delivery is the definitive treatment. Would never be correct in active chorioamnionitis (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Repeat steroids and wait 12h is wrong because steroid benefit takes hours and does not change outcome once intra-amniotic infection is overt; the anti-pattern is delaying delivery to complete steroid courses. Would be correct only in subclinical situations (which is not this case) (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Tocolysis is wrong because tocolysis is contraindicated in chorioamnionitis: it delays definitive treatment and may mask deterioration. Would never be correct in chorioamnionitis (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Amniocentesis first is wrong because the clinical diagnosis (fever, tachycardia, tender uterus, rising CRP, turbid AF) is already established; invasive testing only delays delivery. Would be correct only when subclinical infection needs confirmation and delivery is not yet planned (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: Immediate caesarean is wrong because iatrogenic birth at 27+4 inflicts severe prematurity morbidity (RDS, IVH, NEC, ROP) and does not reverse hypoxic-ischaemic injury already sustained from the co-twin demise; intrauterine transfusion offers the chance to rescue the survivor and prolong gestation. Would be correct only after IUT fails or with further foetal deterioration (RANZCOG; ISUOG).
rationales.C: Weekly outpatient Dopplers is wrong because MCA-PSV >1.5 MoM is a time-critical red flag for foetal anaemia and requires same-day tertiary intervention, not weekly follow-up. Would never be correct with elevated MCA-PSV (RANZCOG; ISUOG).
rationales.D: Maternal IV heparin is wrong because clinically significant maternal coagulopathy after single twin demise is rare in the short term, especially with normal baseline coagulation; the anti-pattern is treating maternal DIC reflexively. Would be correct only with documented developing coagulopathy (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Cord occlusion of the survivor is wrong because the goal after MC co-twin demise is to rescue the surviving healthy twin; terminating it would be unethical and inverts the management logic. Would never be correct in this scenario (RANZCOG; ISUOG).

rationales.A: 20 mcg CHC is wrong because UKMEC categorises migraine with aura as category 4 (unacceptable risk) for any combined hormonal contraceptive regardless of dose; lowering ethinyloestradiol does not eliminate the ischaemic stroke association. Would never be correct in migraine with aura (UKMEC 2016).
rationales.B: 30 mcg CHC with LNG is wrong because UKMEC 4 applies to all CHC formulations in migraine with aura; the progestogen choice does not modify the oestrogen-driven stroke risk. Would never be correct (UKMEC 2016).
rationales.D: Condom-only is wrong because it leaves her with a method that has substantial typical-use failure and offers no more reliable alternative when multiple safer hormonal/non-hormonal options exist (POP, implant, DMPA, LNG-IUS, copper IUD all UKMEC 1-2). Would be correct only if she explicitly preferred barrier-only after counselling (Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Neurology referral first is wrong because migraine with aura is a clinical history diagnosis; a UKMEC 1-2 progestogen-only or non-hormonal method can be started immediately without specialist input. Would be correct only with atypical aura raising concern for vascular event (UKMEC 2016; RACGP).

rationales.A: Urgent MRI first is wrong because when a known dopamine antagonist (risperidone) is the strongly suspected cause, identifying and managing the drug effect should precede imaging; pituitary MRI is reserved for sustained unexplained hyperprolactinaemia off the offending drug. Would be correct only after switching to a prolactin-sparing agent confirms persistence of hyperprolactinaemia (Endocrine Society 2011).
rationales.C: Empirical cabergoline is wrong because dopamine agonists can precipitate or worsen psychosis in patients with schizophrenia and are not first-line when a drug cause is likely; the anti-pattern is treating the lab abnormality without addressing the cause. Would be correct only for prolactinoma confirmed on imaging in stable patients (Endocrine Society 2011).
rationales.D: Macroprolactin screen is wrong because macroprolactin testing is for asymptomatic hyperprolactinaemia; this woman is clinically symptomatic (amenorrhoea, galactorrhoea, low libido), confirming biologically active prolactin. Would be correct only in asymptomatic elevation (Endocrine Society 2011).
rationales.E: Abrupt risperidone cessation is wrong because stopping antipsychotic without psychiatric input risks relapse and self-harm; switches require shared decision-making with the prescribing team. Would never be correct unilaterally (Endocrine Society 2011).

rationales.A: Moderate OHSS labelling is wrong because the haematocrit >=0.45, rising creatinine, oliguria, hypoalbuminaemia and ascites above the umbilicus push her into the severe category; outpatient management would miss thromboembolic and renal complications. Would be correct only with mild ascites and no biochemical derangement (RANZCOG OHSS; ESHRE).
rationales.C: Critical OHSS labelling is wrong because critical OHSS requires anuria, ARDS, thromboembolism or multiorgan failure; she has oliguria not anuria and normal oxygenation. Would be correct only at the critical end of the spectrum, not now (RANZCOG OHSS).
rationales.D: Large-volume saline boluses is wrong because aggressive crystalloid worsens third-spacing and ascites in OHSS; the anti-pattern is treating it as straightforward hypovolaemia. Would be correct in haemorrhagic shock, not OHSS (RANZCOG OHSS; ESHRE).
rationales.E: Withholding thromboprophylaxis is wrong because OHSS is markedly hypercoagulable (raised oestrogen, haemoconcentration, immobility) and early LMWH is recommended; the anti-pattern is treating bleeding risk and clotting risk symmetrically. Would never be correct in severe OHSS (RANZCOG OHSS; ESHRE).

rationales.A: Another 12 months of timed intercourse is wrong because women >=35 with infertility >=6-12 months warrant referral now; spending another year erodes cumulative live-birth probability, especially with low AMH. Would be correct only in women under 35 still inside the 12-month diagnostic window (RANZCOG; NICE CG156).
rationales.C: Three IUI cycles is wrong because IUI offers minimal incremental benefit over expectant management in unexplained infertility with low ovarian reserve at 38, and cycles spent on IUI reduce her usable IVF window. Would be correct only for mild male factor or cervical factor infertility with adequate reserve and younger age (NICE CG156).
rationales.D: Clomiphene alone is wrong because she is already ovulating; clomiphene targets anovulation and does not address her age-related and reserve-related decline. Would be correct only in WHO-II anovulation (e.g. PCOS) (NICE CG156).
rationales.E: Donor oocyte IVF as first line is wrong because at 38 with AMH 6, autologous IVF still has reasonable cumulative live-birth probability; donor oocytes are reserved for very low reserve plus repeated autologous failure or POI. Would be correct only after failed autologous IVF or POI (RANZCOG; NICE).

rationales.B: UAE is wrong because UAE works best for intramural and subserosal fibroids; for a FIGO type 0 (entirely intracavitary, pedunculated) submucosal lesion, embolisation can leave the fibroid necrosing into the cavity with infection. Would be correct for symptomatic intramural fibroids in women not desiring future fertility (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: LNG-IUS is wrong because submucosal fibroids distorting the cavity reduce LNG-IUS efficacy and raise expulsion rates; hysteroscopic resection of the lesion should precede or replace LNG-IUS. Would be correct only after the FIGO 0 fibroid is removed or for HMB without cavity distortion (RANZCOG; NICE).
rationales.D: Total hysterectomy is wrong because she wishes to preserve her uterus and the lesion is well-suited to a uterus-sparing day procedure with high HMB cure rate; hysterectomy is disproportionate first line. Would be correct only after failed conservative management with completed family (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Three months of GnRH agonist as definitive therapy is wrong because GnRH agonists are pre-operative shrinkage agents and bleed-control bridges, not definitive therapy; symptoms return after cessation and bone density limits long-term use without add-back. Would be correct only as pre-operative adjunct, not standalone (RANZCOG).

rationales.A: Reassure and review in 4 months is wrong because infertility is defined as 12 months in women <35 or 6 months in women >=35; this 36-year-old has crossed that threshold and ovarian reserve declines steeply through this age band. Would be correct only in a woman <35 within the 12-month window (RANZCOG; NICE CG156; ASRM).
rationales.C: AMH and ultrasound only, deferring semen analysis, is wrong because male factor accounts for 30-40% of infertility and semen analysis is the single most informative test; deferring delays diagnosis. Would never be correct in initial workup (RANZCOG; ASRM).
rationales.D: Immediate IVF without baseline tests is wrong because baseline tests identify treatable causes (anovulation, tubal blockage, male factor) and guide stimulation protocol choice; IVF without a semen analysis can miss severe male factor needing ICSI. Would be correct only when baseline workup has been completed and IVF chosen (RANZCOG; NICE CG156).
rationales.E: Diagnostic laparoscopy first is wrong because laparoscopy is not first-line workup; non-invasive tests (semen analysis, hormones, HyCoSy/HSG) come first. Would be correct only with strong suspicion of endometriosis or adhesive disease after non-invasive workup (ESHRE Endometriosis 2022; RANZCOG).

rationales.B: Saying AMH <5 confirms POI is wrong because POI requires amenorrhoea or oligomenorrhoea PLUS FSH >25 IU/L on two readings 4 weeks apart in a woman <40; AMH alone does not diagnose POI. Would be correct only after biochemical and clinical POI criteria are met (ESHRE POI Guideline; AMS).
rationales.C: High FSH predicting high yield is wrong because elevated day-3 FSH reflects reduced reserve and predicts poor response to stimulation; the relationship is inverse. Would never be correct (ESHRE POSEIDON; RANZCOG).
rationales.D: AMH predicting years to menopause is wrong because AMH correlates with biological ovarian age at a population level but cannot reliably predict an individual's menopause timing; clinical predictors must be integrated. Would be correct only as population-level prediction, not individual counselling (ESHRE POI Guideline; SREI).
rationales.E: AFC >4 excluding POSEIDON poor-responder is wrong because POSEIDON integrates age, AMH, AFC and previous stimulation response; AFC of 5 with AMH 4 can still fall into poor-prognosis categories. Would never single-handedly exclude POSEIDON classification (ESHRE POSEIDON).

rationales.A: Saying normal day-3 FSH excludes diminished reserve is wrong because high day-3 oestradiol (>250-290 pmol/L) suppresses FSH by negative feedback and falsely normalises the level; FSH cannot be interpreted in isolation. Would be correct only with confirmed low/normal day-3 oestradiol (ESHRE POSEIDON; RANZCOG).
rationales.C: LH:FSH ratio confirming PCOS is wrong because LH:FSH ratios were dropped from PCOS diagnostic criteria; PCOS is diagnosed by Rotterdam criteria (oligo/anovulation, hyperandrogenism, polycystic ovaries on ultrasound or raised AMH). Would never confirm PCOS alone (2023 International PCOS Guideline).
rationales.D: Confirming POI is wrong because POI requires FSH >25 IU/L on two readings 4 weeks apart plus menstrual disturbance in a woman <40; low AMH/AFC alone is diminished reserve, not POI. Would be correct only with biochemical and menstrual POI criteria met (ESHRE POI Guideline; AMS).
rationales.E: Repeat day-3 bloods without changing strategy is wrong because the high oestradiol is the interpretive problem; AMH (cycle-independent) and AFC are the appropriate markers regardless of cycle day. Would be correct only after timing or technique is corrected (ESHRE POSEIDON).

rationales.B: Fasting glucose plus HbA1c alone is wrong because both have lower sensitivity than the 75 g OGTT for detecting impaired glucose tolerance in PCOS, where post-load hyperglycaemia is the dominant abnormality; the 2023 PCOS guideline explicitly prefers OGTT. Would be correct only when OGTT is impractical and an initial low-yield screen is accepted (2023 International PCOS Guideline).
rationales.C: Annual fasting insulin is wrong because the international guideline does not recommend fasting insulin or HOMA-IR for clinical use due to assay variability and lack of management impact; OGTT and lipid screening are the recommended tools. Would be correct only in research settings (2023 International PCOS Guideline).
rationales.D: Cardiac MRI plus stress testing is wrong because PCOS diagnosis does not warrant imaging-based cardiac assessment; risk stratification uses traditional calculators with intensified screening intervals. Would be correct only with symptoms or risk-calculator-driven indication for ischaemia testing (2023 International PCOS Guideline; NHMRC CV risk).
rationales.E: Liver biopsy is wrong because metabolic screening at PCOS diagnosis uses non-invasive markers (LFTs, FIB-4, ultrasound) for NAFLD when suspected; biopsy is reserved for staging significant fibrosis. Would never be correct at initial diagnosis (2023 International PCOS Guideline).

rationales.B: Hysterectomy with BSO is wrong because definitive surgery in a 28-year-old with endometriosis requires completed childbearing and severe refractory symptoms after thorough medical and conservative surgical management; she has not yet had second-line therapy. Would be correct only after exhausting medical and conservative surgical options in a woman who has completed her family (ESHRE Endometriosis 2022; RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Tranexamic acid is wrong because it targets heavy menstrual bleeding rather than endometriosis-associated pain, and continuous COCP suppresses cyclical bleeding anyway. Would be correct only for HMB, not endometriosis pain (ESHRE Endometriosis 2022; RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Empirical IVF is wrong because IVF is for endometriosis-associated infertility (or advanced disease with planned pregnancy), not for pain management in a woman not currently planning conception. Would be correct only when fertility is the goal (ESHRE Endometriosis 2022; RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Long-acting opioids are wrong because chronic opioid use in endometriosis pain causes dependence, hyperalgesia and no disease-modifying effect; the anti-pattern is opioid escalation rather than mechanism-targeted hormonal escalation or laparoscopic excision. Would be correct only as short-term peri-operative analgesia, never chronic (ESHRE Endometriosis 2022).

rationales.B: LNG-IUS is wrong because cavity-distorting submucosal fibroids contraindicate Mirena placement (high expulsion, reduced efficacy) and the LNG-IUS is contraceptive rather than fertility-enhancing in a woman trying to conceive. Would be correct only for HMB without cavity distortion and without conception plans (RANZCOG; NICE NG88).
rationales.C: UAE is wrong because uterine artery embolisation is not recommended for women who wish to conceive due to reduced ovarian reserve from non-target embolisation, raised placental complication risk and uncertain fertility outcome. Would be correct only for women with completed family or those declining surgery (RANZCOG; NICE NG88).
rationales.D: Stopping levothyroxine is wrong because cessation of replacement therapy in known hypothyroidism would produce maternal and foetal hypothyroidism; it does not address the fibroid and is irrelevant to subfertility management. Would never be correct (RANZCOG; AMH).
rationales.E: Long-term GnRH analogue is wrong because GnRH analogues induce hypogonadism and suppress ovulation; they are short-term (3-6 month) pre-operative or bridging agents and are not a fertility-preserving long-term solution. Would be correct only as pre-operative shrinkage before myomectomy (RANZCOG; NICE NG88).

rationales.B: Bisphosphonate first-line is wrong because POI is fundamentally an oestrogen-deficiency state; physiological HRT addresses bone, cardiovascular, vasomotor and genitourinary needs simultaneously, while bisphosphonates only target bone. Would be correct only when HRT is contraindicated or established osteoporosis fails to respond (AMS POI; ESHRE POI Guideline 2024).
rationales.C: Calling COCP equivalent to HRT lifelong is wrong because while COCP is an acceptable alternative (especially when contraception is needed - spontaneous ovulation can occur in POI), physiological HRT generally provides better bone protection; both should continue at least to age 51 rather than truly lifelong. Would be correct only with the COCP-HRT equivalence overstated and the lifelong claim modified (ESHRE POI Guideline 2024; AMS POI).
rationales.D: Defer HRT 6 months is wrong because delay in oestrogen replacement accelerates bone loss, cardiovascular risk and vasomotor/urogenital symptoms; the anti-pattern is treating POI like natural menopause where 'watchful waiting' might be acceptable. Would never be correct (ESHRE POI Guideline 2024; AMS POI).
rationales.E: Topical vaginal oestrogen alone is wrong because vaginal oestrogen treats genitourinary symptoms only with negligible systemic absorption; it provides no bone or cardiovascular protection. Would be correct only as adjunct to systemic HRT for persistent vulvovaginal symptoms (AMS POI).

rationales.A: Continuing COCP is wrong because systolic >=160 or diastolic >=100 is UKMEC category 4 (unacceptable risk) for combined hormonal contraception; waiting 3 months exposes her to stroke/MI risk. Would never be correct at UKMEC 4 levels (FSRH UKMEC 2016).
rationales.C: Switching to patch or ring is wrong because UKMEC categorises all combined hormonal methods (pill, patch, ring) the same in hypertension; the route does not modify oestrogen-driven vascular risk at this BP. Would never be correct (FSRH UKMEC 2016).
rationales.D: Adding antihypertensive and lower-dose COCP is wrong because FSRH guidance is to avoid combined methods if BP is uncontrolled or vascular disease is present; treated hypertension is still UKMEC 3 with adequate control and UKMEC 4 if uncontrolled. Would be correct only when BP is well-controlled in low-risk younger women (FSRH UKMEC 2016).
rationales.E: Tubal ligation as the only option is wrong because progestogen-only LARC methods (implant, LNG-IUS) are highly effective, reversible and UKMEC 1-2 in hypertension; offering sterilisation only is reductive. Would be correct only when she explicitly declines reversible methods (FSRH UKMEC 2016; RANZCOG: Contraception).

rationales.B: Clomiphene is wrong because PPCOS II and subsequent meta-analyses showed letrozole achieves higher ovulation, pregnancy and live birth rates with lower multiple pregnancy risk; clomiphene is now second-line where letrozole is unavailable or fails. Would be correct only if letrozole is contraindicated or inaccessible (2023 International PCOS Guideline; ASRM).
rationales.C: Metformin monotherapy is wrong because it gives lower ovulation, pregnancy and live birth rates than letrozole; it is an adjunct (particularly in BMI >25 or IGT) rather than primary ovulation induction. Would be correct only as adjunct to letrozole or clomiphene (2023 International PCOS Guideline; ASRM).
rationales.D: Injectable gonadotrophins are wrong because they are second-line after oral failure due to higher cost, OHSS risk, multiple-pregnancy risk and need for daily follicular monitoring. Would be correct only after letrozole/clomiphene resistance (2023 International PCOS Guideline; RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Laparoscopic ovarian drilling is wrong because it is reserved for women with clomiphene/letrozole resistance, particularly when laparoscopy is already indicated for another reason; first-line surgical treatment in treatment-naive PCOS would skip the evidence-based oral therapy. Would be correct only as second-line after oral failure (2023 International PCOS Guideline; RANZCOG).

rationales.B: Urgent transsphenoidal surgery is wrong because prolactinomas, unlike other pituitary adenomas, are exquisitely sensitive to medical therapy; dopamine agonists shrink the tumour and restore vision in most cases within weeks. Would be correct only with failed/intolerant dopamine agonist therapy, pituitary apoplexy or rapidly progressive visual loss not responding to medical therapy (Endocrine Society Hyperprolactinaemia 2011).
rationales.C: External beam radiotherapy is wrong because radiotherapy is third-line after failed surgery or for residual aggressive disease; slow onset and risk of hypopituitarism make it unsuitable for treatment-naive prolactinoma. Would be correct only in refractory or aggressive prolactinoma after surgery (Endocrine Society Hyperprolactinaemia 2011).
rationales.D: Observation is wrong because a macroadenoma with visual field defect requires active treatment; chiasmal compression untreated causes permanent visual loss, and dopamine agonists are highly effective. Would be correct only for asymptomatic microprolactinomas with no compressive features (Endocrine Society Hyperprolactinaemia 2011).
rationales.E: COCP alone is wrong because it treats symptomatic amenorrhoea without addressing the tumour or visual compromise, and can mask prolactin monitoring; the anti-pattern is treating the downstream symptom rather than the macroadenoma. Would be correct only for microprolactinomas with stable imaging where contraception/menstrual regulation is the priority (Endocrine Society Hyperprolactinaemia 2011).

rationales.B: Discharge home is wrong because haemoconcentration, oliguria, tense ascites and respiratory compromise meet severe OHSS criteria that mandate inpatient IV management and close monitoring; outpatient management risks VTE, renal failure and rapid deterioration. Would be correct only for mild OHSS with adequate oral intake (RANZCOG OHSS; ESHRE OHSS).
rationales.C: Frusemide infusion is wrong because diuretics in an intravascularly depleted, third-spaced state worsen haemoconcentration and renal dysfunction; volume replacement must precede any diuretic consideration. Would be correct only after intravascular volume is restored and pulmonary oedema persists, not first-line in OHSS (RANZCOG OHSS; ESHRE OHSS).
rationales.D: Laparotomy with oophorectomy is wrong because hyperstimulated ovaries are friable and bleed readily; surgery is reserved for torsion, rupture with significant haemorrhage or ectopic pregnancy. Would be correct only for surgical complications, not for severe OHSS itself (RANZCOG OHSS).
rationales.E: Broad-spectrum antibiotics and antivirals are wrong because the elevated WCC in OHSS reflects the inflammatory state rather than infection; empirical antibiotics are not indicated without clinical evidence of infection. Would be correct only when superimposed infection is clinically suspected (RANZCOG OHSS).

rationales.B: LNG-IUS is wrong because current breast cancer is UKMEC 4 for the LNG-IUS; despite local progestogen action, systemic exposure means it is contraindicated in current/recent breast cancer (UKMEC 3 only when remission is >5 years). Would be correct only after >5 years' disease-free remission and only as UKMEC 3 (FSRH UKMEC 2016).
rationales.C: COCP is wrong because current/recent breast cancer is UKMEC 4 for combined hormonal contraception due to oestrogen-driven recurrence risk; absolute contraindication. Would never be correct in current/recent breast cancer (FSRH UKMEC 2016).
rationales.D: Etonogestrel implant is wrong because UKMEC 4 applies to all progestogen-only methods (implant, POP, DMPA, LNG-IUS) in current breast cancer; the systemic progestogen exposure is unacceptable. Would never be correct in current breast cancer (FSRH UKMEC 2016).
rationales.E: DMPA is wrong because depot progestogen is UKMEC 4 in current breast cancer; all hormonal methods are contraindicated. Would never be correct in current breast cancer (FSRH UKMEC 2016).

rationales.A: COCP is wrong because SLE with positive (or unknown) antiphospholipid antibodies is UKMEC 4 for all combined hormonal contraception due to substantially elevated VTE and arterial thrombosis risk; lowering the oestrogen dose does not change the category. Would never be correct with APL positive (FSRH UKMEC 2016; EULAR Lupus).
rationales.C: Combined ring is wrong because UKMEC 4 applies to all combined methods regardless of route in APL-positive SLE; route does not modify thrombotic risk. Would never be correct in this group (FSRH UKMEC 2016).
rationales.D: DMPA is wrong because it is UKMEC 3 in APL-positive SLE (adverse lipid profile and small but increased VTE association), so an IUD is preferred. Would be correct only when LARC IUDs are not feasible and benefits are judged to outweigh risks (FSRH UKMEC 2016; EULAR Lupus).
rationales.E: Condoms alone is wrong because typical-use failure ~13%/year is inadequate when she has completed her family and seeks reliable contraception; effective LARC is available and appropriate. Would be correct only when she explicitly preferred barrier methods after counselling (FSRH UKMEC 2016; RANZCOG: Contraception).

rationales.B: COCP after RYGB is wrong because the malabsorptive bypass impairs absorption of oral hormonal contraception, making efficacy unreliable regardless of condom backup; FSRH advises against oral methods after malabsorptive bariatric surgery. Would be correct only after restrictive procedures (sleeve gastrectomy, band) where absorption is preserved (FSRH UKMEC 2016; ASMBS/ACOG Bariatric Pregnancy).
rationales.C: POP is wrong because, like the COCP, POP absorption is impaired after Roux-en-Y; the narrow timing window worsens the reliability problem. Would be correct only after restrictive procedures (FSRH UKMEC 2016).
rationales.D: Fertility awareness is wrong because typical-use failure ~24%/year is incompatible with the need for reliable contraception, and post-bariatric menstrual irregularity makes cycle tracking unreliable. Would be correct only as personal preference after counselling about high failure rates (Therapeutic Guidelines: Contraception).
rationales.E: Immediate pregnancy attempts is wrong because ASMBS/ACOG and RANZCOG recommend deferring pregnancy 12-24 months post-bariatric surgery to allow weight stabilisation, micronutrient optimisation (B12, iron, folate, vitamin D) and reduce SGA, prematurity and nutritional deficiency in the foetus. Would be correct only after the 12-24 month interval and nutritional optimisation (ASMBS/ACOG Bariatric Pregnancy; RANZCOG).

rationales.A: Repeating the same 7-day metronidazole alone is wrong because recurrent BV (>=3 episodes/year) needs re-induction PLUS suppressive maintenance therapy; standard courses alone have high recurrence rates due to residual biofilm. Would be correct only for a first or non-recurrent BV episode (Australasian STI Guidelines).
rationales.C: Reassurance is wrong because all four Amsel criteria are met (homogeneous discharge, pH >4.5, positive whiff test, clue cells); symptomatic BV warrants treatment. Would be correct only with normal discharge and no Amsel criteria met (Australasian STI Guidelines).
rationales.D: Empirical partner treatment is wrong because routine treatment of male partners is not currently standard in Australian guidelines (though emerging evidence is evolving the picture); suppressive therapy for the index woman is the established next step. Would be correct only within research protocols or jurisdictions adopting recent partner-treatment data (Australasian STI Guidelines).
rationales.E: IUD removal is wrong because the copper IUD has not been clearly established as a cause of recurrent BV and removal sacrifices effective contraception; suppressive vaginal therapy should be tried first. Would be correct only after suppressive therapy fails with strong temporal IUD association (Family Planning Australia).

rationales.A: Aciclovir 200 mg 5x/day for 3 days is wrong because 200 mg five-times-daily is a recurrence regimen and 3 days is too short; first-episode disease requires 5-10 days of treatment due to higher viral load and slower lesion resolution. Would be correct only for recurrent episodes (ASHM; Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Suppressive 400 mg BD is wrong because suppressive therapy is for frequent recurrences (>=6/year) or partner protection in serodiscordant couples; first episodes need full therapeutic doses. Would be correct only after >=6 recurrences/year or for transmission reduction (ASHM).
rationales.D: Valaciclovir 500 mg BD for 3 days is wrong because that is a recurrence regimen; first-episode valaciclovir dosing is 500 mg BD for 5-10 days. Would be correct only for recurrent episodes (ASHM).
rationales.E: Topical aciclovir is wrong because topical aciclovir does not penetrate adequately and is explicitly not recommended for genital herpes in Australian or international guidelines; systemic therapy is required. Would never be correct for genital herpes (ASHM; BASHH).

rationales.A: Podophyllotoxin is wrong because it is contraindicated in pregnancy due to teratogenicity (antimitotic activity); self-application is doubly inappropriate when she is pregnant. Would be correct only outside pregnancy (Australasian STI Guidelines; Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.B: Imiquimod is wrong because imiquimod has limited pregnancy safety data and is not recommended in pregnancy; the immunomodulatory mechanism creates additional uncertainty. Would be correct only outside pregnancy (Australasian STI Guidelines).
rationales.D: Watch and wait is wrong because anogenital warts may proliferate dramatically during pregnancy and obstruct the birth canal at term; antenatal treatment reduces burden at delivery rather than waiting for postpartum regression. Would be correct only for very small isolated lesions in late pregnancy (Australasian STI Guidelines).
rationales.E: Elective caesarean for HPV is wrong because caesarean is not indicated solely for HPV transmission risk; vaginal delivery is acceptable unless warts physically obstruct the birth canal. Would be correct only with mechanical obstruction at term (Australasian STI Guidelines).

rationales.A: Azithromycin 1 g stat is wrong because Australian guidelines have moved away from single-dose azithromycin to doxycycline for chlamydia, and azithromycin is markedly inferior for rectal infection where treatment failure rates are high. Would be correct only as a second-line option for pregnant women when amoxicillin is unsuitable, not first-line (eTG; ASHM).
rationales.C: Doxycycline 21 days is wrong because that is the LGV regimen; uncomplicated chlamydia (including rectal) is 7 days. Would be correct only for confirmed or strongly suspected lymphogranuloma venereum (Australasian STI Guidelines).
