rationales.D: Amoxicillin is wrong because amoxicillin is reserved for chlamydia in pregnancy where doxycycline is contraindicated; she is not pregnant. Would be correct only in a pregnant patient (eTG).
rationales.E: Ceftriaxone is wrong because ceftriaxone is gonococcal therapy and does not cover chlamydia. Would be correct only for confirmed gonorrhoea (and would still need anti-chlamydia cover) (eTG).

rationales.A: Ciprofloxacin monotherapy is wrong because Australian gonococcal isolates carry widespread quinolone resistance; ciprofloxacin is reserved only when susceptibility is confirmed. Would be correct only with documented quinolone-susceptible isolate (ASHM).
rationales.B: Azithromycin 2 g monotherapy is wrong because macrolide resistance and pharyngeal treatment failure rates are high; the anti-pattern is single-drug therapy in a setting of resistance. Would be correct only when ceftriaxone is contraindicated and susceptibility supports it (ASHM).
rationales.C: Cefixime monotherapy is wrong because oral cefixime has higher MICs and rising resistance; IM ceftriaxone provides more reliable bactericidal pharmacokinetics, especially for pharyngeal infection. Would be correct only when IM access is not possible (ASHM; BASHH).
rationales.E: Benzathine penicillin is wrong because it treats syphilis; Neisseria gonorrhoeae is intrinsically less susceptible and frequently penicillin-resistant. Would be correct only for syphilis, not gonorrhoea (ASHM).

rationales.A: Doxycycline monotherapy is wrong because doxycycline 14 days is reserved for non-pregnant patients with confirmed penicillin allergy; she has no allergy and benzathine penicillin is first-line. Would be correct only with documented penicillin allergy outside pregnancy (ASHM).
rationales.C: Three weekly doses is wrong because that regimen is for late latent syphilis or syphilis of unknown duration (where treponemal eradication takes longer); primary syphilis acquired within 2 years needs only one dose. Would be correct only for late latent or unknown duration syphilis (ASHM).
rationales.D: Procaine penicillin daily 10 days is wrong because that regimen is reserved for neurosyphilis or ocular/otic syphilis when IV benzylpenicillin is impractical; primary syphilis does not need CNS penetration. Would be correct only for neurosyphilis or eye/ear involvement (ASHM).
rationales.E: Ceftriaxone 10 days is wrong because ceftriaxone is an alternative when penicillin is not available; with no allergy, single-dose benzathine penicillin remains first-line. Would be correct only with penicillin allergy or shortage (CDC; ASHM).

rationales.B: Amoxicillin-clavulanate is wrong because it does not reliably cover N. gonorrhoeae or C. trachomatis, the principal pathogens in PID; the anti-pattern is reaching for a broad oral beta-lactam without coverage of the actual culprits. Would never be correct as PID monotherapy (Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Doxycycline monotherapy is wrong because while doxycycline covers chlamydia and atypicals, it does not reliably cover gonorrhoea or anaerobes; PID needs combination therapy. Would be correct only as part of combination (with ceftriaxone and metronidazole), not alone (Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Weekly azithromycin is wrong because single-agent azithromycin is inadequate for PID, risks treatment failure and contributes to macrolide resistance. Would never be correct as PID monotherapy (Therapeutic Guidelines; BASHH).
rationales.E: IV regimen is wrong because IV therapy is reserved for inpatient PID with tubo-ovarian abscess, pregnancy, severe sepsis, failed oral therapy or inability to tolerate oral intake; this woman meets outpatient criteria. Would be correct only with one of those inpatient indications (Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.A: Viral hepatitis A is wrong because acute HAV would produce more dramatic transaminitis (often >1000 U/L) and prodromal/systemic features but would not explain CMT, mucopurulent cervicitis, positive chlamydia NAAT or pleuritic RUQ pain; the constellation of PID plus RUQ pain points to perihepatitis. Would be correct only with isolated severe transaminitis and HAV serology positive (BMJ Best Practice).
rationales.B: Acute cholecystitis is wrong because ultrasound shows no stones, wall thickening or pericholecystic fluid, and Murphy's sign is negative; the combination essentially excludes cholecystitis. Would be correct with characteristic ultrasound findings and positive Murphy's sign (BMJ Best Practice).
rationales.C: Tubo-ovarian abscess is wrong because pelvic ultrasound shows no TOA (trace free fluid is non-specific) and TOAs typically cause severe pelvic, not primarily RUQ, pain. Would be correct only with an imaged abscess and corresponding clinical picture (Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Ruptured ectopic is wrong because serum beta-hCG <5 IU/L excludes pregnancy and therefore ectopic; this is a definitive exclusion. Would never be correct with a negative beta-hCG (RANZCOG).

rationales.A: Vaginal birth plus 2-4 weeks zidovudine is wrong because Australian practice specifies 4 weeks of neonatal zidovudine for low-risk infants of suppressed mothers; the 2-4 week phrasing introduces imprecision and the question demands the precise duration. Would be correct only if the duration were stated as 4 weeks (ASHM; RANZCOG).
rationales.B: Planned caesarean at 39+0 with IV zidovudine is wrong because viral load <50 copies/mL near term obviates the need for caesarean and intrapartum IV zidovudine; vaginal birth is preferred with suppression. Would be correct only with viral load >=50 copies/mL near term (ASHM; RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Planned caesarean plus triple-drug PEP plus breastfeeding is wrong on multiple counts: caesarean is reserved for unsuppressed viral load, triple-drug PEP is for higher-risk infants, and breastfeeding is not recommended in Australia regardless of suppression. Would never be correct in this scenario (ASHM).
rationales.E: Routine intrapartum IV zidovudine plus single-dose nevirapine is wrong because IV intrapartum zidovudine is reserved for unsuppressed/unknown viral load, and single-dose neonatal nevirapine is inadequate cover under Australian standards (oral zidovudine x 4 weeks is the regimen). Would be correct only with unsuppressed viral load and even then the neonatal regimen would differ (ASHM).

rationales.A: Single-dose tenofovir is wrong because PEP requires a 28-day course of combination antiretrovirals; single-dose or monotherapy approaches do not prevent HIV acquisition. Would never be correct as PEP (ASHM PEP guidelines).
rationales.B: Dual NRTI PEP is wrong because Australian guidance now recommends three-drug PEP (TDF/FTC plus dolutegravir or raltegravir) for high-risk exposures; dual NRTI-only PEP is outdated. Would be correct only under older guidelines, not current practice (ASHM).
rationales.C: TAF/FTC/rilpivirine 7 days is wrong because PEP duration is 28 days (not 7) and rilpivirine is not a standard third agent due to drug interactions and resistance considerations. Would never be a recommended PEP regimen (ASHM).
rationales.E: Defer PEP until source viral load is known is wrong because PEP efficacy falls rapidly with time and must start within 72 hours (ideally <24); waiting for source results risks missing the treatment window. Would be correct only if exposure were truly low risk and pending data could rapidly redirect care (ASHM).

rationales.A: Deferring PrEP 4 weeks is wrong because a single recent negative HIV test plus absence of acute HIV symptoms is sufficient to start PrEP; deferring prolongs an unnecessary exposure window. Would be correct only if there are symptoms suggestive of acute seroconversion (ASHM PrEP guidelines).
rationales.B: On-demand (2+1+1) PrEP is wrong because on-demand dosing is only recommended for cisgender MSM with infrequent exposures; cisgender women require daily PrEP due to slower tissue drug levels in cervicovaginal tissue. Would be correct only for selected MSM, not for cisgender women (ASHM).
rationales.D: Defer PrEP because partner is detectable is wrong because a partner with detectable viral load is exactly the indication for PrEP; PrEP is strongly indicated, not contraindicated. Would never be correct (ASHM).
rationales.E: Dolutegravir monotherapy is wrong because dolutegravir is not a PrEP agent; PrEP uses TDF/FTC (daily or, for selected MSM, on-demand). Would never be correct (ASHM).

rationales.A: Defer forensic examination until daylight is wrong because forensic evidence degrades within the <72-hour window; delaying loses DNA, secretions and trace evidence regardless of patient distress (a trained sexual assault examiner is best placed to balance comfort with timeliness). Would never be correct (RANZCOG; Australasian Sexual Health Alliance).
rationales.B: Forensic-only first is wrong because medical priorities (EC within 120 hours, STI prophylaxis, HIV PEP within 72 hours, HBV cover, safety planning) cannot be deferred to await kit processing; they run in parallel with forensic care. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Linking forensic exam to police reporting is wrong because forensic examination should be offered regardless of reporting intent, with anonymous kit storage available to preserve future options; mandating reporting acts as a barrier to disclosure. Would never be correct under Australian best practice (RANZCOG; Australasian Sexual Health Alliance).
rationales.D: Showered as contraindication is wrong because showering reduces but does not eliminate forensic yield; trace DNA and other evidence can still be recovered, and discharging without addressing EC, STI prophylaxis, HIV PEP, HBV cover and safety is unsafe. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: Ruptured ectopic is wrong because serum beta-hCG <5 IU/L definitively excludes pregnancy; an ectopic cannot exist without pregnancy. Would never be correct with beta-hCG negative (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Acute PID is wrong because PID typically presents with bilateral pelvic pain, cervical motion tenderness, mucopurulent discharge and fever, often subacute; sudden severe unilateral pain with abnormal Doppler on a cyst fits torsion. Would be correct only with bilateral CMT, mucopurulent discharge and inflammatory markers (Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Appendicitis is wrong because classical appendicitis produces migratory periumbilical-to-RIF pain with anorexia and low-grade fever; left-sided pain with adnexal Doppler abnormalities points to torsion. Would be correct only with RIF localisation and characteristic features (BMJ Best Practice).
rationales.E: Renal colic is wrong because renal colic produces flank-to-groin pain often with microscopic haematuria; urinalysis is clean and ultrasound localises the abnormality to the ovary with abnormal Doppler. Would be correct only with haematuria and a ureteric stone on imaging (BMJ Best Practice).

rationales.A: No treatment is wrong because symptomatic BV warrants treatment for symptom relief in pregnancy, regardless of whether treatment reduces preterm birth; Australian guidance treats symptomatic BV with oral metronidazole. Would be correct only for asymptomatic BV in low-risk pregnancies (Therapeutic Guidelines; RANZCOG).
rationales.B: Clindamycin vaginal cream is wrong because in the second half of pregnancy the oil-based cream weakens latex condoms and has been associated with adverse neonatal outcomes and preterm birth in some studies; oral therapy is preferred in pregnancy. Would be correct only in the first half of pregnancy when oral metronidazole is unsuitable (Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Tinidazole is wrong because tinidazole has limited pregnancy safety data and is contraindicated in the first trimester; oral metronidazole has the established pregnancy safety profile across trimesters. Would never be correct in pregnancy (Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Metronidazole 2 g single dose is wrong because single-dose 2 g has lower cure rates than 5-7 day regimens for BV; longer courses are preferred in pregnancy. Would be correct only in non-pregnant women preferring single-dose convenience over efficacy (Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.B: Intravaginal metronidazole gel is wrong because topical metronidazole achieves <50% cure for trichomoniasis (parasites in urethra, glands, etc. not reached); systemic therapy is required and partners must be treated to prevent ping-pong reinfection. Would never be correct for trichomonas (Australasian STI Guidelines).
rationales.C: Tinidazole with continued breastfeeding is wrong because tinidazole has a longer half-life requiring an approximately 3-day breastfeeding pause; metronidazole 2 g single dose with a 12-24h pause is preferred in breastfeeding. Would be correct only outside breastfeeding (Australasian STI Guidelines).
rationales.D: Permanently cease breastfeeding is wrong because a brief 12-24h pause is sufficient to clear peak metronidazole levels; permanent cessation is unnecessary and harmful. Would never be correct (Australasian STI Guidelines).
rationales.E: Doxycycline to her only is wrong because doxycycline has no activity against Trichomonas vaginalis and is contraindicated in breastfeeding due to enamel staining; treating her alone also leaves partners to reinfect. Would never be correct for trichomoniasis (eTG; ASHM).

rationales.A: Urgent transvaginal ultrasound is wrong because although TVUS rules out praevia, the 20-week scan was clear and the foetus is acutely distressed; delaying delivery for confirmatory imaging in placental abruption with foetal compromise is unsafe. Would be correct only in stable suspected praevia, not acute abruption with foetal distress (RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage).
rationales.B: IV oxytocin is wrong because oxytocin is contraindicated in a hypertonic, contracting uterus (abruption typically presents with a woody, tense uterus from concealed haemorrhage) and does not address foetal distress; the anti-pattern is augmenting a uterus that is already pathologically contracting. Would never be correct in abruption (RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage).
rationales.D: ARM and internal monitoring is wrong because amniotomy is not appropriate with severe foetal distress, an unengaged foetus and prior caesarean; cervical state and uterine scar limit instrumental rescue. Would be correct only in mild abruption with engaged head, low-risk uterus and reassuring CTG (RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance).
rationales.E: Betamethasone and observe is wrong because corticosteroids are for preterm foetuses (<35 weeks) and this foetus is term at 38 weeks; observation in the face of placental abruption with foetal distress is unsafe. Would be correct only at preterm gestations, not term (KEMH: Corticosteroids in Pregnancy).

rationales.B: Urgent TVUS first is wrong because while imaging is correct for haemodynamically stable women, a shocked patient with positive FAST requires immediate operative management; TVUS would delay laparoscopy and cost the patient blood and time. Would be correct only in stable women with positive beta-hCG and no peritonism (RANZCOG ectopic).
rationales.C: IM methotrexate is wrong because methotrexate requires haemodynamic stability, an unruptured ectopic and low/declining beta-hCG; it is absolutely contraindicated in suspected rupture and shock. Would be correct only for stable ectopic with beta-hCG <5000 and no foetal cardiac activity (RANZCOG ectopic).
rationales.D: IV antibiotics for PID is wrong because the diagnostic picture is shock plus positive pregnancy test plus free fluid - ruptured ectopic until proven otherwise; treating empirically as PID would miss the surgical emergency and likely be fatal. Would be correct only with negative beta-hCG and PID picture (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Repeat beta-hCG in 48h and discharge is wrong because discharging a shocked patient with positive pregnancy and free fluid is reckless and likely fatal; the anti-pattern is treating beta-hCG trending as a substitute for resuscitation. Would never be correct in shock (RANZCOG ectopic).

rationales.B: Immediate caesarean without stabilisation is wrong because operating on an unstabilised patient with severe hypertension, neurological irritability and HELLP coagulopathy risks intra-operative seizure, stroke and uncontrolled haemorrhage; the CTG is reassuring, allowing time to stabilise. Would be correct only after BP control and MgSO4 are running, not as the first step (RANZCOG: Pre-eclampsia).
rationales.C: Expectant management is wrong because severe features (HELLP, severe hypertension, neurological signs) mandate delivery once the mother is stabilised; the anti-pattern is treating severe pre-eclampsia like mild gestational hypertension. Would be correct only in mild pre-eclampsia without severe features at preterm gestations (SOMANZ).
rationales.D: IV diazepam is wrong because magnesium sulfate is the established agent for eclampsia prophylaxis (Collaborative Eclampsia Trial); diazepam is inferior and delays magnesium. Would be correct only when magnesium is contraindicated or unavailable, not as primary prophylaxis (KEMH: Hypertensive Disorders).
rationales.E: Hydralazine alone is wrong because while hydralazine is a valid antihypertensive, omitting magnesium sulfate in the face of clonus (impending eclampsia) ignores the seizure prophylaxis priority. Would be correct only as the antihypertensive component of combined therapy, not alone (SOMANZ).

rationales.A: IV magnesium sulfate is wrong because magnesium is for foetal neuroprotection (<30 weeks preterm labour) or eclampsia prophylaxis; this is post-dates hyperstimulation needing tocolysis. Would be correct only at preterm gestations or for severe pre-eclampsia/eclampsia (KEMH: Intrapartum Care).
rationales.B: Oxytocin augmentation is wrong because the uterus is already hyperstimulated; adding oxytocin would worsen tachysystole and deepen foetal hypoxia. Would never be correct in hyperstimulation (RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance).
rationales.C: Artificial rupture of membranes is wrong because the immediate life-threatening issue is hyperstimulation with prolonged deceleration; ARM does not relieve the contractile state and may worsen cord compression. Would be correct only in non-hyperstimulated labour augmentation (RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance).
rationales.D: Immediate category 1 caesarean is wrong because in-utero resuscitation with acute tocolysis is the first step; tocolysis often restores foetal heart rate within minutes and avoids caesarean. Would be correct only if tocolysis fails or further deterioration occurs (RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance).

rationales.A: Clomiphene is wrong because the 2023 international Monash PCOS guideline supersedes the older clomiphene-first approach; PPCOS II and meta-analyses show letrozole achieves higher live birth rates with lower multiple-pregnancy risk. Would be correct only if letrozole were unavailable or contraindicated (Monash International PCOS Guideline).
rationales.B: Metformin is wrong because metformin improves metabolic parameters but achieves fewer live births than letrozole; it is a second-line or adjunct agent, particularly in BMI >25 or IGT. Would be correct only as adjunct to letrozole or for metabolic indication (Monash International PCOS Guideline).
rationales.C: Gonadotrophin injections are wrong because exogenous FSH in PCOS carries a high OHSS and multiple-pregnancy risk and needs intensive monitoring; strictly second-line. Would be correct only after failed oral ovulation induction (Monash International PCOS Guideline).
rationales.D: Immediate IVF is wrong because IVF is third-line, reserved for women failing pharmacological ovulation induction or with male factor/tubal issues; partner and tubes are normal here. Would be correct only after failed letrozole and IUI cycles (Monash International PCOS Guideline).

rationales.A: IM methotrexate is wrong because methotrexate requires definitive diagnosis of ectopic or confirmed failing PUL where intrauterine viability is excluded; giving it for a PUL risks terminating a viable IUP. Would be correct only after diagnosis is confirmed (RANZCOG: Early Pregnancy Bleeding).
rationales.B: Diagnostic/therapeutic laparoscopy is wrong because surgery is for identified ectopic or haemodynamic instability; this stable patient with no adnexal mass does not warrant invasive surgery. Would be correct only with imaged ectopic or instability (RANZCOG: Early Pregnancy Bleeding).
rationales.C: Surgical D&C is wrong because it would destroy a potentially viable early IUP not yet excluded; chorionic villi sampling can differentiate but is not first-line. Would be correct only after failing trend confirmed and viable IUP excluded (RANZCOG: Early Pregnancy Bleeding).
rationales.D: Oral progesterone is wrong because progesterone has no role in PUL management; it is sometimes used in recurrent miscarriage or in confirmed viable pregnancies. Would be correct only in confirmed viable IUP with bleeding (RANZCOG: Early Pregnancy Bleeding).

rationales.A: 12-month HPV repeat is wrong because HPV 16 (a high-risk genotype) skips the 12-month repeat pathway and goes directly to colposcopy in the Australian NCSP. Would be correct only for non-16/18 oncogenic HPV with negative reflex cytology (National Cervical Screening Program).
rationales.C: 5-yearly screening is wrong because normal cervical appearance does not override a positive HPV 16 result; routine 5-year screening applies only when the current test is low risk. Would be correct only with negative HPV result, not positive HPV 16 (National Cervical Screening Program).
rationales.D: Reflex cytology and await is wrong because reflex cytology is the NCSP pathway for non-16/18 oncogenic HPV, not for HPV 16/18 which go directly to colposcopy. Would be correct only for non-16/18 oncogenic HPV (National Cervical Screening Program).
rationales.E: Defer screening to age 30 is wrong because Australian NCSP starts at age 25 and asymptomatic status does not exempt; her current result is already abnormal. Would never be correct (National Cervical Screening Program).

rationales.A: Escalating oral labetalol and observing is wrong because severe-range hypertension with end-organ features mandates urgent IV stabilisation and delivery planning; oral escalation is too slow. Would be correct only in mild gestational hypertension without severe features (RANZCOG Hypertension in Pregnancy).
rationales.C: Corticosteroids and defer 48h is wrong because severe maternal features override the steroid window after 34 weeks; delivery should not be delayed solely for steroid completion in deteriorating maternal status. Would be correct only at preterm gestations with stable maternal status (KEMH Hypertensive Disorders Guideline).
rationales.D: Urgent caesarean without MgSO4 is wrong because magnesium sulfate is mandatory in severe pre-eclampsia regardless of mode/route of delivery; growth restriction alone does not dictate caesarean either. Would never be correct to skip MgSO4 in severe pre-eclampsia (RANZCOG Hypertension in Pregnancy).
rationales.E: Outpatient review is wrong because sustained severe hypertension and symptoms of impending eclampsia preclude outpatient management; the anti-pattern is treating severe features like mild disease. Would be correct only in mild gestational hypertension with low-risk profile (KEMH Hypertensive Disorders Guideline).

rationales.A: Repeat in 7 days assuming viable IUP is wrong because plateauing beta-hCG above the discriminatory zone with a suspicious adnexal mass is highly suggestive of ectopic; expectant reassessment risks rupture. Would be correct only with rising appropriately doubling beta-hCG and definite IUP on scan (RANZCOG Early Pregnancy Loss).
rationales.C: Emergency laparotomy is wrong because small free fluid can be physiological or limited bleeding; she is haemodynamically stable without peritonism. Would be correct only with shock, large haemoperitoneum or peritonism (KEMH Ectopic Pregnancy Guideline).
rationales.D: D&C to exclude missed miscarriage is wrong because the adnexal mass and plateauing beta-hCG already strongly support ectopic; D&C risks delaying treatment and unnecessary surgery. Would be correct only when discriminating PUL between failing IUP and ectopic with no adnexal mass (RANZCOG Early Pregnancy Loss).
rationales.E: Oral progesterone is wrong because progesterone has no role in suspected ectopic and risks delaying definitive treatment; the anti-pattern is treating any pregnancy bleeding with progesterone reflexively. Would never be correct in suspected ectopic (KEMH Ectopic Pregnancy Guideline).

rationales.A: Admit and observe is wrong because severe-range BP plus symptoms plus thrombocytopenia/renal/liver derangement defines severe pre-eclampsia; observation alone misses the seizure-prophylaxis and stabilisation steps. Would be correct only in mild gestational hypertension without severe features (RANZCOG hypertensive disorders).
rationales.B: Oral labetalol and outpatient review is wrong because severe pre-eclampsia requires inpatient maternal stabilisation; outpatient management is unsafe with severe features. Would be correct only in mild hypertension without severe features (RANZCOG hypertensive disorders).
rationales.D: Terbutaline and tocolyse to 37 weeks is wrong because tocolysis is not the priority in severe pre-eclampsia and prolonging pregnancy at the expense of maternal stability is unsafe; the anti-pattern is treating it like preterm labour. Would never be correct here (RANZCOG hypertensive disorders).
rationales.E: Immediate caesarean without stabilisation is wrong because magnesium sulfate and BP control should precede surgery unless maternal/foetal compromise is imminent; operating on uncontrolled severe hypertension risks intra-operative stroke and eclampsia. Would be correct only in crashing maternal or foetal emergency (RANZCOG hypertensive disorders).

rationales.A: IM carboprost is wrong because carboprost (15-methyl PGF2alpha) is relatively contraindicated in asthma due to bronchospasm risk; in atonic PPH the next uterotonic must avoid her asthma. Would be correct only in non-asthmatic women (RANZCOG Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline).
rationales.B: IV ergometrine is wrong because ergometrine causes vasoconstriction and worsens hypertension; it is relatively contraindicated in hypertensive disorders. Would be correct only in normotensive women (KEMH Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline).
rationales.D: Direct hysterectomy is wrong because medical and tamponade measures should be exhausted in a haemodynamically manageable patient before proceeding to hysterectomy; the anti-pattern is jumping to definitive surgery prematurely. Would be correct only with refractory haemorrhage after all conservative measures (RANZCOG Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline).
rationales.E: Withhold uterotonics for fibrinogen is wrong because uterine atony remains the major bleeding source and must be treated alongside fibrinogen correction; the anti-pattern is sequential rather than parallel resuscitation. Would never be correct as standalone strategy (KEMH Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline).

rationales.A: Continue pushing is wrong because the CTG is pathological with multiple concerning features; ongoing labour risks worsening hypoxia, and the nulliparous-second-stage argument does not justify continued exposure. Would be correct only with reassuring CTG (RANZCOG Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance).
rationales.B: Rotational instrumental delivery is wrong because high station and significant moulding indicate uncertain likelihood of rapid safe vaginal birth; failed instrumental delivery worsens neonatal outcomes. Would be correct only with low station, minimal moulding and confidence of success (RANZCOG Operative Vaginal Birth).
rationales.D: Scalp stimulation is wrong because scalp stimulation helps clarify indeterminate tracings; this is already pathological with foetal tachycardia, minimal variability and recurrent late decelerations - urgent delivery is indicated. Would be correct only for suspicious (not pathological) CTGs (RANZCOG Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance).
rationales.E: Antibiotics and reassess is wrong because antibiotics for chorioamnionitis are necessary but must not delay urgent delivery in pathological CTG; the anti-pattern is treating infection while accepting foetal compromise. Would be correct only as adjunct alongside delivery, not as substitute (RANZCOG Intrapartum Care).

rationales.A: Formal ultrasound first is wrong because placental abruption is primarily a clinical diagnosis; ultrasound has poor sensitivity (only ~25%) and may falsely reassure. With maternal instability and foetal compromise, imaging delays critical intervention. Would be correct only in stable women without compromise (RANZCOG Antepartum Haemorrhage).
rationales.C: Tocolysis and steroids is wrong because tocolysis is contraindicated in significant abruption with bleeding and foetal compromise; delivery cannot wait for steroid completion. Would be correct only in mild abruption with stable mother and reassuring CTG at preterm gestation (KEMH Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline).
rationales.D: ARM and induction is wrong because the foetus is compromised and the mother haemodynamically unstable; induction would be too slow and unsafe. Would be correct only in mild abruption with stable mother, term gestation, favourable cervix and reassuring CTG (RANZCOG Antepartum Haemorrhage).
rationales.E: Observation only is wrong because vaginal bleeding can underestimate concealed haemorrhage in abruption; maternal shock and pathological CTG mandate delivery. Would be correct only in mild, stable abruption with reassuring CTG (KEMH Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline).

rationales.A: Normal OGTT is wrong because under ADIPS criteria a single abnormal value diagnoses GDM; the fasting threshold (>=5.1 mmol/L) is met regardless of post-load values. Would be correct only if all three values were below threshold (ADIPS Consensus Guidelines).
rationales.C: Indeterminate, repeat at 32 weeks is wrong because diagnostic criteria are already met on this OGTT; repeat testing is unnecessary and delays management. Would be correct only with borderline values not meeting any threshold (ADIPS Consensus Guidelines).
rationales.D: Pre-existing T2DM is wrong because overt diabetes in pregnancy requires fasting >=7.0 mmol/L or HbA1c >=6.5% or random >=11.1 mmol/L; her fasting 5.4 is well below those thresholds. Would be correct only with much higher fasting glucose or elevated HbA1c (RANZCOG Gestational Diabetes).
rationales.E: Impaired fasting glucose label is wrong because Australian pregnancy-specific ADIPS criteria classify this as GDM, not the non-pregnancy IFG category; pregnancy thresholds differ. Would be correct only outside pregnancy (ADIPS Consensus Guidelines).

rationales.A: IM ergometrine is wrong because ergometrine is contraindicated in hypertension and is suboptimal in asthma; the patient's comorbidities exclude it. Would be correct only in normotensive non-asthmatic women (RANZCOG postpartum haemorrhage).
rationales.B: IM carboprost is wrong because carboprost can precipitate bronchospasm in asthma; her asthma makes it relatively contraindicated. Would be correct only in non-asthmatic women (RANZCOG postpartum haemorrhage).
rationales.D: IM oxytocin 10 IU is wrong because she has already received intrapartum oxytocin and is actively haemorrhaging; the priority is escalation (tranexamic acid plus alternative uterotonic plus mechanical measures) rather than repeating oxytocin alone. Would be correct only as initial prophylaxis, not in established refractory PPH (RANZCOG postpartum haemorrhage).
rationales.E: Immediate hysterectomy is wrong because surgical escalation is reserved for refractory bleeding after medical therapy fails; jumping to hysterectomy first skips reversible interventions including tranexamic acid. Would be correct only as last resort after all conservative measures fail (RANZCOG postpartum haemorrhage).

rationales.A: Continue expectancy for steroid completion is wrong because suspected chorioamnionitis mandates delivery regardless of steroid status; ongoing infection drives maternal sepsis and foetal injury. Would be correct only with no infection signs and stable preterm PPROM (RANZCOG Preterm Birth).
rationales.C: Tocolysis is wrong because tocolysis is contraindicated in suspected intra-amniotic infection (delays delivery, masks deterioration); the anti-pattern is treating preterm uterine activity as the threat when infection is. Would never be correct in chorioamnionitis (KEMH PPROM Guideline).
rationales.D: Repeat ultrasound and observe is wrong because maternal fever, uterine tenderness and raised inflammatory markers make chorioamnionitis far more likely than isolated dehydration; the anti-pattern is treating infection diagnosis as a uncertainty problem rather than clinical-syndrome recognition. Would never be correct in this clinical picture (RANZCOG Preterm Birth).
rationales.E: Stop antibiotics, blame steroids is wrong because while steroids transiently affect WCC, the overall clinical picture is consistent with infection; stopping antibiotics in suspected chorioamnionitis worsens maternal and foetal outcomes. Would never be correct in this picture (KEMH PPROM Guideline).

rationales.A: 12-month repeat is wrong because HPV 16 positivity triggers direct colposcopy in the Australian NCSP regardless of cytology; the anti-pattern is treating HPV 16 like an intermediate-risk result. Would be correct only for non-16/18 HPV with negative reflex cytology (Cancer Council Australia Cervical Screening).
rationales.C: 5-year repeat is wrong because a previous normal result does not override current HPV 16 detection; the screening interval applies only when current result is low risk. Would be correct only with current negative HPV result (NCSP Guidelines).
rationales.D: Immediate excisional treatment is wrong because HPV 16 increases risk but does not confirm histologic high-grade disease; treatment decisions require colposcopy and biopsy. Would be correct only after histological HSIL confirmation (Cancer Council Australia Cervical Screening).
rationales.E: HPV vaccination booster first is wrong because vaccination is preventive and does not alter management of current HPV 16 positivity; colposcopy is required regardless of vaccination status. Would never substitute for colposcopy (NCSP Guidelines).

rationales.B: Repeat beta-hCG only is wrong because serial hCG is helpful when initial ultrasound is non-diagnostic, but in a stable patient with hCG in the discriminatory zone (~1500 IU/L for TVUS), TVUS provides direct visualisation. Would be correct only when initial ultrasound is uninformative (RANZCOG early pregnancy guidance).
rationales.C: D&C is wrong because there is no confirmed intrauterine pregnancy loss; uterine evacuation without localising the pregnancy risks destroying a viable IUP or missing an ectopic. Would be correct only after failing PUL is confirmed (RANZCOG early pregnancy guidance).
rationales.D: CT with contrast is wrong because TVUS is the appropriate first-line imaging in early pregnancy and avoids ionising radiation exposure to a potentially viable pregnancy. Would be correct only if non-pregnancy intra-abdominal pathology dominates and ultrasound is non-diagnostic (RANZCOG early pregnancy guidance).
rationales.E: Discharge with reassurance is wrong because pain and bleeding plus positive beta-hCG require exclusion of ectopic before discharge; the anti-pattern is treating early pregnancy bleeding as low risk without localisation. Would never be correct without exclusion of ectopic (RANZCOG early pregnancy guidance).

rationales.A: Anti-D 250 IU is wrong because from 13 weeks onwards the NBA sensitising-event dose is 625 IU IM; 250 IU is the pre-12-week dose. Would be correct only before 13 weeks (NBA Anti-D Ig Guidelines).
rationales.C: 1500 IU is wrong because the 1500 IU dose is reserved for routine antenatal prophylaxis at 28 and 34 weeks (the dual-dose schedule); sensitising events use 625 IU at 13+ weeks. Would be correct only as 28/34-week routine prophylaxis (NBA).
rationales.D: Withholding because cervix closed is wrong because sensitising events after 12 weeks require anti-D regardless of bleeding pattern or cervical state; foeto-maternal haemorrhage can occur silently. Would never be correct (NBA).
rationales.E: Withholding because of last year's dose is wrong because anti-D protection lasts approximately 12 weeks; protection from a year ago has long since lapsed. Would never be correct (NBA).

rationales.A: Stronger downward traction is wrong because excessive traction is the principal cause of brachial plexus injury in shoulder dystocia; the anti-pattern is overpowering rather than disimpacting. Would never be correct (RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia).
rationales.C: Fundal pressure is wrong because fundal pressure worsens impaction by driving the anterior shoulder onto the symphysis and risks uterine rupture and worsened brachial plexus injury; suprapubic pressure (downward and lateral) is the correct adjunct. Would never be correct (RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia).
rationales.D: Immediate episiotomy is wrong because shoulder dystocia is a bony impaction (anterior shoulder behind pubic symphysis); episiotomy facilitates internal manoeuvres but does not itself relieve impaction. Would be correct only as adjunct to internal manoeuvres, not as first step (RCOG Shoulder Dystocia Guideline).
rationales.E: Direct posterior arm delivery is wrong because basic manoeuvres (McRoberts plus suprapubic pressure) resolve most shoulder dystocias and should be tried first; foetal bradycardia is an expected feature of shoulder dystocia, not a marker of failed first-line manoeuvres. Would be correct only after first-line manoeuvres fail (RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia).

rationales.A: Methotrexate is wrong because methotrexate is contraindicated in haemodynamic instability and suspected rupture; the criterion is stable patient with unruptured ectopic, low/declining hCG. Would be correct only in stable unruptured ectopic with appropriate hCG (KEMH Ectopic Pregnancy Guideline).
rationales.B: Repeat TVUS is wrong because the patient is in haemorrhagic shock with clinical evidence of rupture; delaying for imaging would be fatal. Would be correct only in haemodynamically stable women with unclear initial scan (RANZCOG Early Pregnancy Loss).
rationales.D: Observation after fluids is wrong because intraperitoneal bleeding plus haemodynamic instability defines ongoing rupture; spontaneous resolution is not a viable strategy when the patient is shocked. Would never be correct in shock (RANZCOG Early Pregnancy Loss).
rationales.E: CT for appendicitis is wrong because the clinical picture (positive pregnancy test, shock, intraperitoneal free fluid) overwhelmingly indicates ruptured ectopic; CT delays surgery and exposes a potentially viable pregnancy elsewhere to radiation. Would be correct only if pregnancy is excluded and appendicitis is the leading differential (KEMH Ectopic Pregnancy Guideline).

rationales.A: Oral antihypertensives plus expectancy is wrong because severe pre-eclampsia features (neurological symptoms, severe BP, thrombocytopenia, transaminitis) need IV control plus magnesium and delivery planning; oral escalation is inadequate. Would be correct only for mild gestational hypertension without severe features (SOMANZ Hypertension Guideline).
rationales.C: Steroids and delay 7 days is wrong because at 35 weeks maternal severe disease outweighs prematurity considerations; the anti-pattern is treating maternal severe disease as a trade-off for foetal lung maturity at near-term gestations. Would be correct only at earlier preterm gestations with stable maternal status (RANZCOG Hypertensive Disorders).
rationales.D: Outpatient review tomorrow is wrong because severe pre-eclampsia features mandate inpatient management with magnesium sulfate; the anti-pattern is using foetal wellbeing alone to justify outpatient care. Would never be correct with severe features (SOMANZ Hypertension Guideline).
rationales.E: IV antibiotics for sepsis is wrong because the pattern (HELLP-like with neurological symptoms and severe BP) supports severe pre-eclampsia, not sepsis; thrombocytopenia and transaminitis fit HELLP. Would be correct only with clinical or microbiological evidence of infection (RANZCOG Hypertensive Disorders).

rationales.A: Immediate hysterectomy is wrong because uterine atony usually responds to medical management (oxytocin, ergometrine, carboprost, misoprostol, tranexamic acid) and tamponade (balloon, B-Lynch); the anti-pattern is skipping reversible interventions. Would be correct only with refractory bleeding after all conservative measures fail (RANZCOG Postpartum Haemorrhage).
rationales.C: Observe for spontaneous contraction is wrong because ongoing heavy bleeding with haemodynamic compromise demands active resuscitation and uterotonics; consciousness does not equal stability. Would never be correct in active PPH (RWH Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline).
rationales.D: CT angiography for retained products is wrong because the immediate postpartum boggy uterus is atony until proven otherwise; imaging delays critical intervention. Would be correct only after atony is treated and retained products remain suspected (RANZCOG Postpartum Haemorrhage).
rationales.E: Antibiotics for endometritis is wrong because endometritis typically presents days postpartum with fever and pelvic pain, not immediate massive haemorrhage from a boggy uterus; the anti-pattern is treating PPH as a sepsis problem. Would never be correct in primary PPH (RWH Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline).

rationales.B: Active pushing is wrong because at 6 cm dilation with foetal bradycardia and prolapsed cord, vaginal delivery is impossible; pushing accelerates cord compression and worsens hypoxia. Would never be correct in cord prolapse with incomplete dilation (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Manual cord replacement is wrong because manipulation triggers umbilical vessel spasm and arterial vasoconstriction; the cord is left in situ while the presenting part is elevated. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Repeat digital VE is wrong because repeated examinations stimulate the cord, worsen vasospasm and delay theatre transfer; the focus is presenting-part elevation, not progress assessment. Would never be correct in cord prolapse (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Wrap cord and await resolution is wrong because warm saline gauze can be applied if cord is exposed externally, but spontaneous resolution is not a viable strategy; immediate operative delivery is mandatory. Would be correct only as adjunct alongside immediate caesarean transfer (RANZCOG).

rationales.A: Maternal age over 35 is wrong because advanced maternal age modestly increases praevia and accreta risk, but the magnitude is far less than that conferred by praevia plus prior uterine surgery; the strongest predictor is the combination. Would be correct only when comparing age effects alone, not against the praevia-plus-CS interaction (RANZCOG: Placenta Accreta Spectrum).
rationales.B: Persistent major praevia at 28 weeks is wrong because while persistent praevia raises PAS risk, isolated praevia without prior caesarean carries only about 3% PAS risk; the dominant driver is the combination with prior caesareans. Would be correct only as one component, not the strongest single predictor (RANZCOG: Placenta Accreta Spectrum).
rationales.C: Suction curettage plus prior caesareans is wrong because while curettage adds minor endometrial disruption, the magnitude of risk is dwarfed by the praevia-plus-multiple-caesarean combination. Would be correct only if asked about additive minor risks, not the strongest predictor (RANZCOG: Placenta Accreta Spectrum).
rationales.E: Drop in haemoglobin is wrong because the haemoglobin fall reflects current bleeding severity, not PAS risk; PAS risk is determined by pre-existing uterine factors. Would never be correct as a PAS risk factor (RANZCOG: Placenta Accreta Spectrum).

rationales.A: Firmer downward traction is wrong because excessive traction causes brachial plexus injury without disimpacting the anterior shoulder behind the symphysis; the anti-pattern is using force rather than disimpaction manoeuvres. Would never be correct (RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia).
rationales.C: Rubin I (external abdominal pressure on anterior shoulder) is wrong because Rubin I is essentially a form of suprapubic pressure and has already been attempted; after external manoeuvres fail, internal rotational manoeuvres or posterior arm delivery are indicated. Would be correct only before suprapubic pressure has been tried (RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia).
rationales.D: Episiotomy is wrong because shoulder dystocia is a bony impaction, not soft-tissue obstruction; episiotomy facilitates access for internal manoeuvres but does not itself relieve impaction. Would be correct only as adjunct to internal manoeuvres, not as a standalone step (RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia).
rationales.E: Gaskin all-fours is wrong because with epidural in situ, all-fours positioning is difficult and potentially unsafe; in an Australian tertiary setting with epidural, internal manoeuvres are the next step. Would be correct only without effective regional anaesthesia or as an earlier alternative in unmedicated labour (RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia).

rationales.B: 5-minute load plus 2 g/hr is wrong because a 5-minute load risks maternal hypotension and arrest; SOMANZ specifies 20-minute infusion. The 2 g/hr maintenance also exceeds the Australian 1 g/hr standard, increasing magnesium toxicity risk without added benefit. Would never be correct in Australian practice (SOMANZ: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy).
rationales.C: IM Pritchard regimen is wrong because IM-only therapy is painful with unreliable absorption and is rarely used in contemporary Australian tertiary settings with IV access; IV is the standard route. Would be correct only in resource-limited settings without IV infusion capacity (SOMANZ).
rationales.D: 2 g IV load is wrong because 2 g is sub-therapeutic for established eclampsia; the eclampsia regimen requires 4 g loading. Would be correct only for severe pre-eclampsia seizure prophylaxis, not for treating an established seizure (SOMANZ).
rationales.E: 6 g load plus 48 hours is wrong because 6 g exceeds the 4 g standard and 48 hours exceeds the 24-hour standard (24h post-delivery or post-last-seizure, whichever later); both increase toxicity without benefit. Would never be correct in Australian practice (SOMANZ).

rationales.A: IM syntometrine is wrong because syntometrine is typically used for third-stage prophylaxis rather than treating established PPH, contains ergometrine (risk of induced hypertension in a woman with normal BP receiving oxytocin), and is suboptimal in asthma. Would be correct only as third-stage prophylaxis in a non-asthmatic, non-hypertensive woman (KEMH PPH; Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetric Emergencies).
rationales.B: IM carboprost is wrong because carboprost (PGF2-alpha) is relatively contraindicated in asthma due to bronchospasm risk; even well-controlled asthma makes it a poor choice when ergometrine is available. Would be correct only in non-asthmatic women, particularly after ergometrine fails (Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetric Emergencies; KEMH PPH).
rationales.C: TXA plus ergometrine is wrong because the question asks for a single uterotonic next; TXA is an antifibrinolytic adjunct, not a uterotonic, and is given alongside uterotonics rather than as substitute. Would be correct as combination therapy, not as the single uterotonic choice (WOMAN Trial; KEMH PPH).
rationales.E: Rectal misoprostol is wrong because rectal absorption is slow and inferior to parenteral routes in acute PPH with IV access; misoprostol is reserved for when other uterotonics are contraindicated or unavailable. Would be correct only when IV/IM routes are not accessible or when prior agents are contraindicated (KEMH PPH).

rationales.A: Azithromycin plus ampicillin-amoxicillin is wrong because Australian first-line PPROM latency cover is erythromycin alone (per ORACLE I); the azithromycin/ampicillin combination is not standard, and the temperature of 37.9 degC needs serial observation rather than tipping straight to extensive antibiotics. Would be correct only when erythromycin is contraindicated or unavailable (RANZCOG: PPROM Guideline; KEMH: PPROM Guideline).
rationales.B: Immediate oxytocin induction is wrong because a borderline temperature of 37.9 degC alone, with no uterine tenderness, reassuring CTG and no purulent discharge, does not diagnose chorioamnionitis or mandate delivery; serial observations and inflammatory markers are required first. Would be correct only with confirmed chorioamnionitis or significant deterioration (RANZCOG: PPROM Guideline; KEMH: PPROM Guideline).
rationales.D: Single betamethasone and discharge is wrong because outpatient PPROM management is not recommended due to risk of ascending infection, cord prolapse and placental abruption; inpatient monitoring is the standard. Would never be correct as discharge plan (RANZCOG: PPROM Guideline).
rationales.E: Ampicillin/clarithromycin and deliver at 37+0 is wrong because the latency antibiotic regimen should be erythromycin (co-amoxiclav is avoided due to NEC risk per ORACLE I), and delivery is typically planned by 34+0 in Australian practice rather than 37+0. Would be correct only if erythromycin were unavailable and the patient remained stable, with revised antibiotic and delivery timing (KEMH: PPROM Guideline; RANZCOG: PPROM Guideline).

rationales.A: No IAP, treat infant if symptomatic is wrong because the purpose of IAP is preventing early-onset GBS disease before clinical signs develop; waiting for infant signs misses the prevention window. Would be correct only with very low risk and confirmed negative GBS (RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy).
rationales.B: IAP for all unknown-status labour is wrong because IAP for unknown status is reserved for those with risk factors (preterm labour <37, ROM >=18h, temp >=38, prior GBS infant, GBS bacteriuria); this woman currently has none. Would be correct only if risk factors emerged (RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy).
rationales.C: Swab in labour and treat if positive is wrong because culture results will not be available in time to guide intrapartum decision-making; the anti-pattern is using a too-slow test as a decision tool. Would be correct only as preconception or antenatal screening, not as intrapartum decision-making (RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy).
rationales.E: Cefazolin for unknown status with no risk factors is wrong because cefazolin is reserved for non-severe penicillin allergy with confirmed indication for IAP; she has no allergies and no current indication for IAP. Would be correct only with non-severe penicillin allergy and an indication for IAP (RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy).

rationales.A: Delivery within 24 hours is wrong because while delivery is indicated, the 24-48 hour timeframe is appropriate when DV a-wave is positive and there are no CTG decelerations; insisting on within 24 hours overstates urgency. Would be correct only if DV a-wave were abnormal or recurrent decelerations developed (RANZCOG: Foetal Growth Restriction Guideline; KEMH).
rationales.C: Immediate caesarean within 2-4 hours is wrong because AEDF with reduced variability but positive DV a-wave does not meet criteria for emergency category-1 delivery; immediate delivery is for recurrent decelerations, reversed end-diastolic flow or abnormal DV a-wave. Would be correct only with abnormal DV a-wave or recurrent decelerations (RANZCOG: FGR Guideline).
rationales.D: Repeat CTG in 12 hours is wrong because AEDF plus reduced variability is itself an indication for delivery; expectant management beyond 48 hours after these features develop is unsafe. Would be correct only at earlier surveillance phases before AEDF develops (RANZCOG: FGR Guideline).
rationales.E: Rescue steroids and delay to 32 weeks is wrong because the prior course was only the day before, so a rescue course is not indicated; furthermore, AEDF plus reduced variability mandates delivery within 24-48 hours, not delay to 32 weeks. Would be correct only if >7 days had elapsed since last course AND foetal surveillance were reassuring (RANZCOG: FGR Guideline).

rationales.A: Placental abruption is wrong because abruption presents with painful bleeding, uterine tenderness and a hypertonic uterus; this woman has painless bleeding after ROM in the context of velamentous cord insertion with a sinusoidal CTG, fitting vasa praevia. Would be correct only with the painful-bleeding-and-woody-uterus triad (RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline; KEMH).
rationales.B: Placenta praevia is wrong because the 32-week scan confirmed placental clearance of the os; recurrent praevia is unlikely, and praevia bleeding is maternal not foetal (would not cause a sinusoidal CTG unless massive maternal haemorrhage caused hypotension, which is absent here). Would be correct only with confirmed low-lying placenta and maternal hypotension (RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline).
rationales.D: Uterine rupture is wrong because rupture causes acute severe abdominal pain, loss of presenting part, maternal haemodynamic instability and often catastrophic CTG change; this woman is pain-free, stable, with intact uterus. Would be correct only with the rupture triad and prior uterine surgery (RANZCOG: Uterine Rupture Guideline).
rationales.E: Feto-maternal haemorrhage from placental lake rupture is wrong because FMH typically presents with persistent foetal bradycardia or sinusoidal pattern WITHOUT visible vaginal bleeding (foetal blood enters maternal circulation); visible bleeding after ROM with velamentous cord insertion points strongly to vasa praevia. Would be correct only if there were no visible vaginal bleeding with foetal anaemia (RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline).

rationales.A: Adnexal mass 2.4 cm is wrong because mass size <3.5-4 cm is a criterion supporting methotrexate (not excluding it); 2.4 cm is well within the acceptable size range. Would be correct only if mass were >=4 cm (RANZCOG: Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy; KEMH).
rationales.B: Beta-hCG 2850 with suboptimal rise is wrong because beta-hCG <5000 is a criterion for methotrexate; suboptimal rise confirms non-viable pregnancy and supports rather than excludes medical management. Would be correct only with beta-hCG >5000 or rapidly rising (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Tubal ring without yolk sac is wrong because that ultrasound appearance is classic tubal ectopic ('bagel sign'); absence of yolk sac/foetal pole is common and absence of cardiac activity is in fact a positive predictor for methotrexate success. Would be correct only if a viable foetal pole with cardiac activity were present (KEMH).
rationales.D: Normal renal/hepatic/platelets is wrong because normal blood count, creatinine and transaminases are prerequisites for methotrexate (it is renally excreted and hepatically metabolised); they support, not exclude, medical management. Would be correct only if abnormal (Therapeutic Guidelines: Ectopic Pregnancy; AMH: Methotrexate).

rationales.A: Dinoprostone 3 mg tablet (Prostin E2) is wrong because in Australian practice the standard first-line agent for primigravidae with unfavourable cervix is dinoprostone 2 mg gel 6-hourly (max 2 doses); the tablet regimen has different dosing and is not the standard first-line. Would be correct only with the appropriate tablet schedule and where tablets are local practice (KEMH: Induction of Labour Guideline).
rationales.C: Vaginal misoprostol 25 mcg is wrong because Australian first-line for unfavourable cervix in low-risk primigravidae is dinoprostone gel; misoprostol may be used in specific settings (PPROM, intrauterine death) but is not the established first-line agent. Would be correct only in those specific settings or where local protocols specify misoprostol (KEMH).
rationales.D: Foley catheter is wrong because mechanical ripening is reserved for women with contraindications to prostaglandins (prior caesarean, severe asthma); this low-risk primigravida is suitable for prostaglandin. Would be correct only when prostaglandins are contraindicated (KEMH).
rationales.E: Oxytocin without ripening is wrong because oxytocin is less effective with unfavourable cervix (Bishop <=6); skipping ripening increases failed induction and caesarean rates. Would be correct only with favourable cervix (Bishop >=7) (RANZCOG: Induction of Labour Guideline).

rationales.A: IV fluid resuscitation plus prophylactic LMWH plus daily ward review is wrong because oliguria despite 6 kg weight gain, haemoconcentration and tense abdomen indicate moderate-to-severe OHSS requiring paracentesis for tense ascites and improved renal perfusion; supportive measures alone miss the volume-shift problem. Would be correct only in mild-moderate OHSS without tense ascites or renal impairment (RANZCOG: OHSS; KEMH: OHSS Guideline).
rationales.C: Therapeutic LMWH is wrong because while OHSS is hypercoagulable, therapeutic anticoagulation is reserved for clinical thromboembolism; prophylactic dosing is standard for moderate-severe OHSS and therapeutic dose adds bleeding risk after oocyte retrieval. Would be correct only with confirmed VTE (RANZCOG: OHSS).
rationales.D: Discharge on cabergoline is wrong because cabergoline is a PREVENTIVE agent for OHSS in high-risk women, not a treatment for established moderate-severe OHSS with oliguria and ascites; the anti-pattern is treating established disease with prevention measures. Would be correct only as antenatal OHSS prophylaxis in high-risk IVF stimulation (RANZCOG: OHSS).
rationales.E: Diagnostic laparoscopy for torsion is wrong because bilateral enlargement with ascites and systemic signs is classic OHSS, not torsion; surgical intervention in friable hyperstimulated ovaries with coagulopathy risk would be hazardous. Would be correct only with acute severe unilateral pain plus Doppler evidence of torsion (KEMH: OHSS Guideline).

rationales.B: Basal insulin first-line is wrong because ADIPS supports metformin as first-line oral pharmacotherapy when lifestyle fails, before moving to insulin; insulin is second-line or used alongside metformin if targets are not met. Would be correct only if metformin were contraindicated or refused (ADIPS: GDM Guideline).
rationales.C: Continue lifestyle 2 more weeks is wrong because ADIPS recommends pharmacotherapy if targets are not achieved within 1-2 weeks of lifestyle; she has had elevated fasting BGLs for 3 weeks. Would be correct only at the 1-2 week mark with marginal elevation (ADIPS: GDM Guideline).
rationales.D: Glibenclamide is wrong because it is not recommended first-line in Australia: it crosses the placenta and is associated with higher rates of macrosomia and neonatal hypoglycaemia compared to metformin or insulin. Would be correct only in very limited resource-poor settings, not Australian practice (ADIPS: GDM Guideline; RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Metformin plus insulin simultaneously is wrong because dual therapy as initial pharmacological step is not indicated; metformin alone should be trialled first with insulin added only if targets remain unmet after dose escalation. Would be correct only with severe baseline derangement requiring rapid control (ADIPS: GDM Guideline).

rationales.A: Amoxicillin/metronidazole/gentamicin triple therapy is wrong because the Australian first-line for postpartum endometritis is clindamycin plus gentamicin; triple therapy is reserved for severe intra-abdominal sepsis or specific concerns. Would be correct only with severe sepsis or polymicrobial intra-abdominal source (Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetric Infections; KEMH).
rationales.C: Ceftriaxone plus metronidazole is wrong because it lacks adequate gram-positive coverage (streptococci, enterococci) needed for pelvic infection; clindamycin gives the gram-positive and anaerobic coverage. Would be correct only in selected pyelonephritis or when clindamycin contraindicated (Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetric Infections).
rationales.D: Piperacillin-tazobactam monotherapy is wrong because while broad-spectrum, it is reserved for severe sepsis or suspected resistant organisms; standard postpartum endometritis is treated with clindamycin plus gentamicin. Would be correct only with severe sepsis or specific resistance concerns (Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Amoxicillin-clavulanate monotherapy is wrong because anaerobic coverage may be inadequate for severe pelvic infections and it is not the Australian guideline regimen. Would be correct only as oral step-down in mild infection, not as IV first-line (Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetric Infections).

rationales.B: Repeat HPV in 12 months is wrong because HPV 16 positivity with any cytology abnormality (including pLSIL/LSIL) requires direct colposcopy in the NCSP, regardless of age; conservative testing misses high-risk progression. Would be correct only for non-16/18 HPV with negative reflex cytology (NCSP: Management of Screen-detected Abnormalities).
rationales.C: Repeat cytology in 6 months is wrong because once HPV 16 is detected, repeat cytology alone is insufficient; HPV 16 plus any cytology abnormality triggers colposcopy. Would be correct only when HPV is negative with LSIL cytology (NCSP).
rationales.D: Colposcopy only if symptomatic is wrong because NCSP referral criteria are not symptom-dependent; HPV 16 plus pLSIL is an indication regardless of symptoms. Would never be correct (NCSP).
rationales.E: Empirical LLETZ without colposcopy is wrong because LLETZ is treatment for histologically confirmed HSIL/CIN2-3; performing it for pLSIL on cytology alone is overtreatment. Would be correct only after colposcopic biopsy confirms HSIL (NCSP; Cancer Council Australia Cervical Screening Guidelines).

rationales.A: Dark vs bright red blood is wrong because blood colour can occur in both abruption and praevia (older blood appears darker in either condition); colour alone is not a reliable discriminator. Would never be a primary discriminator (RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline).
rationales.C: Hypertension and smoking risk factors is wrong because both are risk factors for abruption AND praevia (smoking is also a praevia risk factor); risk factors do not differentiate the acute diagnosis as strongly as clinical signs do. Would be correct only as supporting evidence, not the strongest differentiator (RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline).
rationales.D: Pathological CTG is wrong because pathological tracings occur in both conditions (abruption from placental separation, praevia from maternal hypotension or cord compression); CTG abnormality indicates compromise but not cause. Would never be a primary differentiator (RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance).
rationales.E: Ultrasound showing placenta clear of os is wrong because while it excludes praevia, it does not confirm abruption (ultrasound is negative in up to 50% of abruptions); the question asks for a finding that favours abruption, not one that excludes praevia. Would be correct only as a praevia rule-out (RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline).

rationales.B: Continue oral labetalol to 39 weeks is wrong because pre-eclampsia at term (>=37 weeks) is itself an indication for delivery; the PHOENIX trial showed reduced maternal morbidity with planned 37-week delivery vs expectant management. Would be correct only for gestational hypertension without pre-eclampsia (SOMANZ: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy; PHOENIX Trial).
rationales.C: Immediate caesarean for severe pre-eclampsia is wrong because she does not meet severe pre-eclampsia criteria (BP <170/110, platelets normal, creatinine normal, transaminases normal, no symptoms, PCR 45 mg/mmol is significant proteinuria but does not by itself denote severe disease); vaginal delivery via induction is appropriate. Would be correct only with severe features (SOMANZ).
rationales.D: Discharge with delivery at 40+0 is wrong because pre-eclampsia at term mandates delivery, not outpatient observation; discharging risks deterioration outside monitoring. Would never be correct in pre-eclampsia at term (SOMANZ; Safer Care Victoria: Timing of Birth).
rationales.E: Wait until 38+0 for full steroid effect is wrong because steroids are not indicated at >=37 weeks (lungs are mature) and waiting prolongs pre-eclampsia unnecessarily. Would be correct only at preterm gestations (SOMANZ; RANZCOG: Antenatal Corticosteroids Guideline).

rationales.A: Suspicious classification and wait 30 min is wrong because tachycardia >160 plus reduced variability <5 plus late decelerations meets pathological criteria, not suspicious; continuing oxytocin in pathological CTG worsens hypoxia. Would be correct only for a suspicious CTG (one major feature without ongoing decelerations) (RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Guideline).
rationales.B: Pathological with maternal O2 and wait 30 min is wrong because maternal oxygen has not been shown to improve foetal outcomes and is not recommended by RANZCOG; furthermore waiting 30 min after a pathological tracing with no response to scalp stimulation is too long. Would be correct only if maternal hypoxia were itself the cause (RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Guideline).
rationales.D: Suspicious due to pyrexia/epidural and continue oxytocin is wrong because while maternal pyrexia/epidural can contribute to foetal tachycardia, the reduced variability, late decelerations and lack of scalp-stimulation acceleration go beyond simple maternal effects; continuing oxytocin in pathological tracing is unsafe. Would be correct only with isolated mild tachycardia attributable to pyrexia (RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Guideline).
rationales.E: Immediate caesarean without intrauterine resuscitation is wrong because intrauterine resuscitation (cease oxytocin, reposition, tocolysis) should be initiated while preparing for theatre; many tracings recover and avoid unnecessary surgery. Would be correct only with acute prolonged bradycardia or terminal bradycardia (RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Guideline).

rationales.A: Antibiotics plus oxytocin alone is wrong because the open os with visible tissue strongly indicates retained products of conception; antibiotics and uterotonics without addressing retained tissue do not solve the bleeding source. Would be correct only for endometritis without retained products (KEMH: Secondary PPH Guideline).
rationales.B: Digital exploration without ultrasound is wrong because blind digital exploration risks uterine perforation, especially in a tender, infected postpartum uterus; confirmation with ultrasound and evacuation under anaesthesia in theatre is safer. Would be correct only in an emergency without ultrasound access (KEMH: Secondary PPH Guideline).
rationales.D: TXA, ergometrine, misoprostol then wait 4h is wrong because while these may temporarily reduce bleeding, they do not address retained products; delay risks ongoing haemorrhage and worsening sepsis. Would be correct only as bridging while awaiting theatre (KEMH: Secondary PPH Guideline).
rationales.E: Oral antibiotics and outpatient ultrasound is wrong because she is systemically unwell (tachycardic, hypotensive, febrile, anaemic) with ongoing haemorrhage; outpatient management is unsafe. Would be correct only in mild secondary endometritis without sepsis or significant bleeding (Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetric Emergencies).

rationales.B: Cease metformin and start basal-bolus is wrong because basal insulin alone is appropriate when fasting hyperglycaemia is the primary problem; basal-bolus is for failed postprandial control on optimised basal. Metformin should be continued alongside insulin to reduce insulin requirements. Would be correct only with severe postprandial spikes despite optimised basal (ADIPS: GDM Guideline).
rationales.C: Increase metformin and add glibenclamide is wrong because she is at maximum metformin (2 g/day) and glibenclamide is not recommended in Australia (higher neonatal hypoglycaemia, macrosomia); escalation should be to insulin. Would be correct only outside Australian guideline practice (ADIPS).
rationales.D: Continue current regimen and reassess in 2 weeks is wrong because ADIPS recommends escalation if targets are not met within 1-2 weeks of maximal oral therapy; fasting BGLs have been above target for 3 weeks with EFW already on the 82nd centile, raising macrosomia risk. Would be correct only at the 1-2 week mark with marginal elevation (ADIPS).
rationales.E: Prebreakfast bolus only is wrong because her fasting BGLs (5.3-5.8) are consistently above the 5.1 target, driving macrosomia; fasting hyperglycaemia should be tackled with basal insulin first. Would be correct only if fasting were at target and postprandial were the primary issue (ADIPS).

rationales.B: Plan vaginal breech after failed ECV is wrong because the Term Breech Trial showed reduced perinatal morbidity and mortality with planned caesarean vs planned vaginal breech delivery; vaginal breech remains a possibility only with rigorous selection and experienced operator, and primigravidity is a relative risk factor. Would be correct only in carefully selected cases with experienced clinician after counselling (RANZCOG: Management of Breech Presentation at Term).
rationales.C: Skip ECV entirely is wrong because RANZCOG recommends offering ECV to all women with breech at term in the absence of contraindications; ECV is worthwhile (35-40% success in primigravidae) and risks are low (foetal HR changes 5%, abruption <1%, emergency CS <0.5%). Would be correct only with contraindications to ECV (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Immediate ECV at clinic without tocolysis is wrong because ECV should be performed in a setting with immediate caesarean access, tocolysis (e.g. terbutaline 250 mcg SC), informed consent and appropriate facilities; clinic ECV without tocolysis lowers success and safety. Would never be correct in this setting (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Wait until 39 weeks for reassessment is wrong because spontaneous version after 37 weeks occurs in only ~8% of breech; waiting misses the optimal ECV window and risks labour with undiagnosed plan. Would be correct only when ECV has already failed and a delivery plan is in place (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: Withhold steroids because beyond 34 weeks is wrong because she is 33+5 (below 34+0), inside the standard window; the misclassification skips clear benefit. Would be correct only at >=34+6 with no late-preterm criteria (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Single dose is wrong because the complete course is two doses 24 hours apart; single doses have reduced benefit. Would be correct only when delivery is so imminent that the second dose cannot be given (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Weekly repeats until delivery is wrong because routine weekly repeats are not recommended due to reduced birth weight and head circumference; only a single rescue dose is considered when delivery is anticipated within 7 days and the first course was >=7 days ago. Would be correct only as a single rescue dose, not weekly (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Dexamethasone 4-dose regimen is wrong because Australian practice predominantly uses betamethasone; while dexamethasone is an equivalent alternative, the question is whether to give ACS not which agent, and the option implies a regimen change without grounds. Would be correct only when betamethasone is unavailable (RANZCOG).

rationales.A: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate outpatient is wrong because she has features of sepsis (HR 108, T 38.7, WCC 16.2, CRP 110) requiring admission and IV antibiotics; the anti-pattern is treating septic endometritis as a UTI-equivalent. Would be correct only in mild post-discharge endometritis without sepsis features (eTG; RANZCOG postpartum infection).
rationales.C: Oral doxycycline is wrong because doxycycline is for PID outpatient regimen, inadequate for septic postpartum endometritis, and contraindicated in breastfeeding due to enamel staining. Would be correct only in non-breastfeeding outpatient PID (eTG).
rationales.D: Ceftriaxone monotherapy is wrong because it misses anaerobic cover for pelvic infection; combination therapy is needed (or alternative regimen with metronidazole). Would be correct only with metronidazole added (eTG).
rationales.E: Withhold antibiotics for ultrasound is wrong because in sepsis, empirical antibiotics must be started within the first hour (Sepsis Six); imaging follows but does not delay treatment. Would never be correct in sepsis (Sepsis Six / eTG).

rationales.A: Elective 48-72 hour delay is wrong because severe features (hepatic involvement, symptoms) mandate delivery within 24-48 hours of completing steroids, not deliberate elective delay; the anti-pattern is treating severe pre-eclampsia like late-preterm mild disease. Would be correct only with mild features not severe (SOMANZ Guideline).
rationales.B: Deliver within 24 hours regardless is wrong because at 29 weeks, the 24-48 hour window for steroid completion is justified unless there is severe refractory hypertension, eclampsia, pulmonary oedema or abruption; the foetal benefit of steroid completion is substantial. Would be correct only with severe refractory features (SOMANZ Guideline).
rationales.C: Expectancy to 34 weeks is wrong because expectant management at <34 weeks is reserved for the absence of severe features; she has hepatic involvement and symptoms, which mandate delivery after steroid completion. Would be correct only in mild pre-eclampsia without severe features (SOMANZ Guideline).
rationales.E: Immediate caesarean for HELLP is wrong because HELLP requires haemolysis (LDH >600, low haptoglobin), ALT >70 and platelets <100; her platelets are 130, so HELLP is not fully met. Furthermore, severe pre-eclampsia after steroid course allows vaginal delivery route if appropriate. Would be correct only with full HELLP and rapidly deteriorating maternal status (SOMANZ Guideline).

rationales.A: Monitor and deliver only if bile acids >40 is wrong because her bile acids are already 58 (>=40) and rising; delaying to threshold rather than at threshold misses the stillbirth-prevention window. Would never be correct given current levels (RANZCOG: Cholestasis in Pregnancy; RCOG Green-top 43).
rationales.B: Plan delivery at 39+0 is wrong because bile acids >=40 carry increased stillbirth risk, and delivery at 39 weeks exposes the foetus to that risk; RANZCOG/RCOG recommend 37 weeks for moderate ICP. Would be correct only for mild ICP (bile acids <40) (RANZCOG; RCOG).
rationales.D: Elective caesarean at 37+0 is wrong because there is no evidence supporting routine caesarean for cholestasis; vaginal delivery via induction is standard. Would be correct only with obstetric indications for caesarean (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Dexamethasone to reduce bile acids and delay to 38+0 is wrong because dexamethasone has not been shown to be effective in ICP and UDCA is first-line; delaying to 38+0 exposes the foetus to stillbirth risk. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: Tocolysis before initial replacement is wrong because the first attempt should be immediate (Johnson manoeuvre), with tocolytics (terbutaline, GTN) as adjunct if initial reduction fails; the anti-pattern is delaying replacement to fetch drugs and theatre. Would be correct only if initial replacement fails (RANZCOG: Uterine Inversion).
rationales.C: Remove placenta first is wrong because removing the placenta from the inverted uterus exposes the placental bed and dramatically worsens haemorrhage; placenta is replaced with the uterus then removed afterwards. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Hydrostatic reduction (O'Sullivan) first is wrong because manual replacement (Johnson) is the immediate first step; hydrostatic reduction is an alternative if manual replacement fails. Would be correct only as second-line (KEMH: Uterine Inversion Guideline).
rationales.E: Transfer to theatre for laparotomy first is wrong because laparotomy (Huntington/Haultain procedures) is reserved for cases where manual reduction (under anaesthesia) fails; immediate vaginal attempt is the first step. Would be correct only after failed manual reduction under anaesthesia (RANZCOG).

rationales.A: Pulmonary embolism is wrong because PE causes hypoxia and collapse but not the profound consumptive coagulopathy (fibrinogen <0.5, low platelets, raised D-dimers) seen here; AFE's hallmark is the DIC component. Would be correct only in pure PE without DIC (RANZCOG: Amniotic Fluid Embolism; UKOSS Study).
rationales.C: Septic shock from chorioamnionitis is wrong because chorioamnionitis typically presents with fever, uterine tenderness and purulent liquor before collapse, and the DIC develops more gradually; the catastrophic sudden onset with rapid DIC fits AFE. Would be correct only with prodromal chorioamnionitis (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Anaphylaxis is wrong because anaphylaxis does not typically cause the extreme consumptive coagulopathy with fibrinogen <0.5; bronchospasm, urticaria and angioedema would be expected. Would be correct only with classic anaphylactic features and triggering exposure (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Placental abruption is wrong because abruption typically presents with abdominal pain, uterine hypertonicity and vaginal bleeding; sudden respiratory distress and severe DIC without uterine signs favours AFE. Would be correct only with the painful-bleeding-and-woody-uterus picture (RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline).

rationales.A: No surveillance, conceive once hCG undetectable is wrong because monthly hCG surveillance is needed for at least 6 months after normalisation to detect persistent trophoblastic disease; conception during this window confounds interpretation. Would never be correct after complete mole (RANZCOG: Gestational Trophoblastic Disease).
rationales.C: Hysterectomy is wrong because routine hysterectomy is not required for complete mole in a young woman who desires future fertility; surveillance with hCG manages persistent disease risk, and hysterectomy is reserved for older women or invasive mole. Would be correct only in older women with completed family or invasive disease (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Single-dose methotrexate prophylactically is wrong because prophylactic chemotherapy is not routine for complete mole; most patients resolve with evacuation alone. Would be correct only for selected high-risk profiles (very high hCG, age >40, very large uterus) (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: CT staging routinely is wrong because routine CT is not performed for uncomplicated complete mole with falling hCG; staging is reserved for persistent/rising hCG or clinical suspicion of metastases. Would be correct only with persistent disease or metastatic symptoms (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: Oophorectomy via laparotomy is wrong because absent Doppler flow does not reliably predict non-viability; many ovaries recover after detorsion even when discoloured. Ovarian conservation is standard of care. Would be correct only with frankly necrotic non-viable ovary (RANZCOG: Adnexal Masses in Pregnancy).
