rationales.C: Observation and analgesia is wrong because torsion does not resolve spontaneously and delay results in ovarian necrosis; urgent surgical detorsion is required. Would never be correct (KEMH: Ovarian Torsion Guideline).
rationales.D: CT with contrast is wrong because CT is relatively contraindicated in pregnancy due to radiation exposure, the diagnosis is clinical/ultrasound-based, and imaging delays surgery. Would be correct only if there were a non-pregnancy diagnostic dilemma and ultrasound failed (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Cyst aspiration is wrong because aspiration does not detorse the pedicle and may cause rupture, bleeding or infection; surgical detorsion is the only effective treatment. Would never be correct in torsion (KEMH: Ovarian Torsion Guideline).

rationales.B: Withhold ACS at <23 weeks is wrong because updated RANZCOG/ANZNN guidance supports ACS from 22+0 weeks when active resuscitation is planned, given evidence of reduced neonatal mortality; the anti-pattern is using outdated 23+0 cutoff. Would be correct only at <22+0 weeks or when palliative neonatal care is chosen (RANZCOG; ANZNN).
rationales.C: Single dose is wrong because the complete course is two doses 24 hours apart; partial courses have reduced effect though some benefit if delivery is imminent. Would be correct only if delivery occurred before the second dose could be given (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Dexamethasone 6 mg q6h is wrong because the standard regimen is 6 mg IM 12-hourly for four doses; q6h is too frequent and not evidence-based. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Defer until membranes rupture is wrong because ACS is given when preterm birth is anticipated within 7 days; waiting for ROM loses the lead time required for steroid effect on lung maturation. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: Karyotypes plus thrombophilia screen is wrong because parental karyotyping is not routine in Australia (offered only with family history of chromosomal disease) and inherited thrombophilia screening (other than APS) is not recommended because it does not alter management. Would be correct only with relevant family history (RANZCOG: Recurrent Miscarriage).
rationales.C: FBC, TFTs, glucose, OGTT is wrong because these are general health screens, not specific causes of recurrent miscarriage; while TFTs are often included, fasting glucose and OGTT are not standard recurrent-miscarriage workup. Would be correct only as general health check, not specific workup (RANZCOG: Recurrent Miscarriage).
rationales.D: Ovarian reserve markers is wrong because AMH, FSH, oestradiol and AFC assess reserve and IVF prognosis, not causes of recurrent loss; ovarian reserve does not explain her three losses. Would be correct only for fertility planning, not recurrent miscarriage workup (RANZCOG: Recurrent Miscarriage).
rationales.E: MTHFR and inherited thrombophilias is wrong because MTHFR testing is not recommended (no clinical management implications) and factor V Leiden/PT mutation testing is not routine in Australian recurrent-miscarriage workup. Would be correct only in selected research/case-specific contexts (RANZCOG: Recurrent Miscarriage).

rationales.B: Placental abruption is wrong because abruption typically causes pain plus woody-hard hypertonic uterus rather than LOSS of contractions; the prior caesarean, oxytocin and loss of contractions point to scar rupture. Would be correct only with hypertonic uterus and no prior surgery (RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage).
rationales.C: Cord prolapse is wrong because cord prolapse presents with a palpable cord protruding and variable decelerations, not constant pain or bleeding; the picture is wrong. Would be correct only with cord visible at the introitus and variable decelerations (RANZCOG: Cord Prolapse).
rationales.D: Normal labour pain with anxiety is wrong because CTG bradycardia plus bleeding excludes normal labour; the anti-pattern is dismissing intrapartum red flags as anxiety. Would never be correct here (RANZCOG: VBAC Guideline).
rationales.E: AFE is wrong because AFE causes acute hypoxia, cardiovascular collapse and DIC, not isolated uterine pain and bleeding; the clinical picture is different. Would be correct only with the cardiovascular-respiratory-DIC triad (RANZCOG: Amniotic Fluid Embolism).

rationales.B: Fasting glucose only is wrong because fasting alone misses impaired glucose tolerance (post-load hyperglycaemia); ADIPS specifies 75 g OGTT post-partum. Would be correct only as adjunct, not standalone (ADIPS: GDM Guideline).
rationales.C: HbA1c is wrong because HbA1c is less sensitive for detecting diabetes in the early postpartum period (red cell turnover, gestational haemodilution); OGTT is preferred. Would be correct only later as part of ongoing surveillance, not first postpartum screen (ADIPS).
rationales.D: No test until age 40 is wrong because women with GDM carry a substantially elevated lifetime T2DM risk (up to 50% over 10 years) and require regular postpartum and ongoing screening; waiting to age 40 misses early diagnosis. Would never be correct (ADIPS).
rationales.E: Home glucose monitoring is wrong because formal OGTT is the standard postpartum screening tool; home monitoring has no role in screening. Would never be correct as the screening test (ADIPS).

rationales.B: Diagnostic laparoscopy now is wrong because primary dysmenorrhoea is a clinical diagnosis and laparoscopy is reserved for refractory pain after NSAID and COCP trials, or features suggestive of secondary dysmenorrhoea. Would be correct only after failed medical management or red flags for endometriosis (RANZCOG; ESHRE).
rationales.C: Paracetamol plus codeine is wrong because opioids are not first-line in adolescent dysmenorrhoea (dependence risk, no disease-modifying effect) and NSAIDs have stronger evidence. Would never be correct as first-line (eTG).
rationales.D: GnRH agonist is wrong because GnRH agonists in adolescents risk bone density loss and are not used without confirmed endometriosis with add-back HRT planning; NSAIDs are first-line. Would be correct only in confirmed refractory endometriosis (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: TENS as first-line is wrong because TENS is a useful adjunct with modest evidence but not first-line pharmacotherapy; NSAIDs remain first-line. Would be correct only as adjunct (RANZCOG; Cochrane).

rationales.B: Transvaginal ultrasound is wrong because TVUS detects ovarian endometriomas and some deep infiltrating endometriosis but cannot exclude superficial peritoneal disease; the gold standard remains laparoscopy. Would be correct only as screening adjunct, not gold standard (RANZCOG: Endometriosis).
rationales.C: MRI pelvis is wrong because MRI is useful for mapping deep infiltrating endometriosis (bowel/bladder/rectovaginal) but is not the diagnostic gold standard; laparoscopy with visualisation and biopsy remains gold standard. Would be correct only as a pre-operative mapping tool (RANZCOG: Endometriosis).
rationales.D: CA-125 is wrong because CA-125 has poor sensitivity and specificity for endometriosis and can be elevated in many other conditions; it is not diagnostic. Would never be diagnostic (RANZCOG: Endometriosis).
rationales.E: Empirical GnRH agonist is wrong because GnRH agonist provides symptomatic improvement but does not give histological diagnosis and carries significant adverse effects; the anti-pattern is treating diagnostic uncertainty with hormonal suppression. Would be correct only as therapeutic trial after diagnosis, not as the diagnostic test (RANZCOG: Endometriosis).

rationales.B: Induction at 37 weeks for SPD is wrong because delivery does not improve outcomes and induces iatrogenic prematurity morbidity; SPD typically resolves postnatally without delivery escalation. Would be correct only for standard obstetric indications, not SPD itself (KEMH: Pelvic Girdle Pain).
rationales.C: Strict bedrest is wrong because bedrest leads to deconditioning, VTE risk and does not improve pain; the anti-pattern is treating SPD as a structural injury requiring immobilisation. Would never be correct (KEMH: Pelvic Girdle Pain).
rationales.D: Intra-articular corticosteroid injection is wrong because injections are reserved for severe refractory cases after conservative measures have failed; not first-line. Would be correct only after physiotherapy and analgesia fail (KEMH: Pelvic Girdle Pain).
rationales.E: Regular oxycodone is wrong because opioids are avoided in pregnancy (foetal CNS depression, neonatal abstinence) and not first-line for SPD; paracetamol plus non-pharmacological measures are preferred. Would be correct only as short-course rescue for severe pain after conservative measures (KEMH: Pelvic Girdle Pain).

rationales.B: Indomethacin 7 days is wrong because indomethacin risks foetal ductus arteriosus constriction and oligohydramnios in the third trimester; while it has anti-inflammatory effect on degeneration, safer analgesia is preferred. Would be correct only before 30 weeks when third-trimester risks do not yet apply (eTG; RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Antenatal myomectomy is wrong because it carries high risk of haemorrhage and foetal loss; reserved for refractory torted pedunculated fibroids. Would be correct only with torsion of a pedunculated fibroid or refractory necrotic lesion (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Caesarean for abruption is wrong because abruption presents with bleeding, woody hypertonic uterus and foetal distress; her CTG is reassuring and pain is localised, not the abruption picture. Would be correct only with confirmed abruption (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Broad-spectrum antibiotics for septic fibroid is wrong because mild inflammatory marker rise is expected in red degeneration; without sepsis or chorioamnionitis features, antibiotics are not indicated. Would be correct only with sepsis or infection (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: Serial TVUS with ultrasound-indicated cerclage is wrong because that strategy is preferred when there is ONE prior preterm birth/loss; with two or more typical mid-trimester insufficiency losses, history-indicated cerclage at 12-14 weeks is recommended. Would be correct only with one prior preterm birth (RANZCOG cerclage).
rationales.C: Vaginal progesterone alone is wrong because progesterone is indicated for asymptomatic short cervix on TVS or one prior preterm birth, but does not replace history-indicated cerclage in classical insufficiency. Would be correct only as adjunct or alternative when cerclage is declined/contraindicated (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Bed rest is wrong because bedrest has no evidence of benefit and increases VTE risk; it cannot substitute for cerclage. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Rescue cerclage only when dilation occurs is wrong because rescue cerclage carries higher complication risk and lower success; planned history-indicated cerclage at 12-14 weeks is superior in classical insufficiency. Would be correct only as salvage when history-indicated cerclage was missed or declined (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: Category 1 caesarean is wrong because caesarean is reserved for maternal indications (e.g. praevia with bleeding, abruption with maternal compromise); after IUFD, vaginal birth is preferred and avoids surgical risk. Would be correct only with maternal indications for caesarean (RANZCOG stillbirth).
rationales.C: Discharge and await spontaneous labour at term is wrong because awaiting term risks DIC from prolonged retention; induction is recommended. Would never be correct as default (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Betamethasone and delay 48h is wrong because corticosteroids have no foetal benefit after demise; the delay raises DIC risk without benefit. Would never be correct after IUFD (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Repeat scan in 24h is wrong because diagnosis is already confirmed by ultrasound (typically by two clinicians per protocol); delay prolongs distress and DIC risk. Would never be correct once diagnosis is confirmed (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: Tocolysis is wrong because tocolysis is contraindicated in abruption with maternal compromise and foetal distress; immediate delivery is required. Would never be correct in this scenario (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Cannulae and wait 30 min is wrong because resuscitation runs concurrently with delivery preparation; waiting 30 minutes worsens outcomes for mother and foetus. Would never be correct as isolated step (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: ARM to expedite vaginal birth is wrong because vaginal birth in abruption requires stable mother and foetus and advanced labour; with shock and bradycardia, caesarean is mandatory. Would be correct only with stable mother, stable foetus and advanced labour, none of which apply (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Transfer for tertiary input is wrong because she is haemodynamically unstable and the foetus is in extremis; transfer is unsafe and immediate on-site delivery is required. Would be correct only in a stable patient at non-tertiary site (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: Continue oxytocin is wrong because continuing oxytocin in late decelerations worsens uteroplacental insufficiency by increasing uterine activity and reducing perfusion; the anti-pattern is maintaining augmentation in the face of foetal distress. Would never be correct in late decelerations (RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Guideline).
rationales.C: Immediate caesarean without resuscitation is wrong because normal variability and baseline mean there is time for intrauterine resuscitation; many tracings recover and avoid unnecessary surgery. Would be correct only if intrauterine resuscitation fails or with prolonged bradycardia (RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Guideline).
rationales.D: Scalp electrode and FBS is wrong because scalp blood sampling is used if CTG remains pathological after resuscitation, not as first step; the immediate response is intrauterine resuscitation. Would be correct only as second step after failed resuscitation (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Maternal oxygen and increased oxytocin is wrong because maternal oxygen has not been shown to improve foetal outcomes and is not recommended; increasing oxytocin would worsen the situation. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: Reassurance of baby blues is wrong because baby blues resolve within 2 weeks; symptoms beyond 2 weeks with functional impairment define postpartum depression. Would be correct only within the first 2 weeks postpartum without functional impact (RANZCOG: Perinatal Mental Health).
rationales.C: Immediate inpatient mother-baby admission is wrong because inpatient admission is reserved for severe depression, suicidal ideation or postpartum psychosis with safety concerns; this is moderate depression without active risk. Would be correct only with severe risk features (RANZCOG: Perinatal Mental Health).
rationales.D: Fluoxetine plus stop breastfeeding is wrong because fluoxetine has a long half-life and higher milk:plasma ratio, making sertraline preferred in breastfeeding; stopping breastfeeding is unnecessary on appropriate SSRI. Would be correct only when sertraline fails or is contraindicated (RANZCOG: Perinatal Mental Health).
rationales.E: Lifestyle measures only is wrong because exercise and social engagement are adjuncts but insufficient for moderate depression (EPDS 13-19); active treatment with therapy and medication is recommended. Would be correct only for mild symptoms or as adjunct to active treatment (RANZCOG: Perinatal Mental Health).

rationales.B: Direct surgical curettage is wrong because surgical management is one option among three (expectant, medical with misoprostol, surgical) with comparable safety in stable women; mandating surgery ignores patient preference. Would be correct only when the patient prefers surgical management or with active heavy bleeding (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Methotrexate for possible ectopic is wrong because TVUS confirms intrauterine pregnancy products and beta-hCG is falling appropriately, making ectopic unlikely. Would never be correct here (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: IV oxytocin to expedite expulsion is wrong because oxytocin is not standard for first-trimester miscarriage; misoprostol is the medical option of choice for first-trimester pregnancy loss. Would be correct only for second/third-trimester losses with viable uterine response (eTG).
rationales.E: Discharge with no follow-up is wrong because follow-up beta-hCG to negative confirms resolution and excludes retained products or molar pregnancy; the anti-pattern is treating falling hCG as complete resolution without confirmation. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: IV ampicillin/gentamicin for 48h is wrong because that regimen is for established chorioamnionitis or proven infection, not for uncomplicated PPROM latency; the question is about latency therapy. Would be correct only with clinical chorioamnionitis (RANZCOG: PPROM Guideline).
rationales.C: Co-amoxiclav is wrong because the ORACLE I trial showed increased neonatal necrotising enterocolitis with co-amoxiclav in PPROM; it is explicitly avoided. Would never be correct in PPROM latency (ORACLE I Trial).
rationales.D: No antibiotics is wrong because PPROM <34 weeks routinely receives latency antibiotics regardless of fever status; afebrile presentation does not exclude subclinical infection. Would be correct only at term PROM, not preterm (RANZCOG: PPROM Guideline).
rationales.E: IM ceftriaxone monotherapy is wrong because ceftriaxone is not the established Australian PPROM latency regimen; erythromycin is. Would be correct only when erythromycin is contraindicated and ceftriaxone is selected as alternative under specialist advice (RANZCOG: PPROM Guideline).

rationales.A: Wait 48 hours for full steroid course is wrong because HELLP with severe features (low platelets, raised AST, raised LDH, haemolysis) is rapidly progressive and delivery should not be delayed beyond 24 hours; the anti-pattern is treating HELLP like mild pre-eclampsia where steroid completion is feasible. Would be correct only in mild pre-eclampsia without HELLP (SOMANZ).
rationales.C: Second dose at 6 hours is wrong because steroid doses are 24 hours apart (or minimum 12 hours when needed); giving at 6 hours does not optimise neonatal benefit and the rush is not necessary. Would be correct only with truly imminent delivery within hours (SOMANZ).
rationales.D: Expectant 48 hours with MgSO4 is wrong because HELLP with foetal compromise carries high risk of eclampsia, hepatic rupture and abruption; expectancy beyond 24 hours is not recommended even with platelets >50. Would never be correct in HELLP with foetal compromise (SOMANZ).
rationales.E: Immediate caesarean under GA is wrong because immediate delivery is reserved for acute deterioration, eclampsia or non-reassuring CTG unresponsive to resuscitation; close monitoring with expedited delivery within 12-24 hours is reasonable. Would be correct only with acute decompensation, not now (SOMANZ).

rationales.B: Stop MgSO4 and give calcium gluconate plus phenytoin is wrong because the magnesium level is therapeutic not toxic, so calcium gluconate (the antidote) is not indicated; phenytoin is less effective than magnesium in eclampsia. Would be correct only with documented magnesium toxicity (absent reflexes, respiratory depression) (SOMANZ).
rationales.C: Midazolam plus CT head is wrong because the immediate management of recurrent eclamptic seizure is an additional magnesium bolus, not benzodiazepine; CT head is reserved for focal neurology or atypical features. Would be correct only with focal signs raising suspicion of intracranial haemorrhage (SOMANZ).
rationales.D: Immediate caesarean is wrong because the immediate priority is maternal seizure control with magnesium; delivery (the definitive treatment) is expedited once stabilised. Would be correct only after stabilisation (SOMANZ).
rationales.E: Labetalol to lower BP is wrong because while BP control matters, the immediate priority is stopping the seizure with magnesium; the BP target in pre-eclampsia is not <140/90 but rather <160/110 to maintain placental perfusion. Would be correct only after seizure control as adjunct (SOMANZ).

rationales.A: Deliver within 24 hours is wrong because with positive DV a-wave and no CTG decelerations, delivery can be planned within 24-48 hours (not strictly within 24); the option is overly aggressive on timing. Would be correct only with abnormal DV a-wave or recurrent decelerations (RANZCOG: FGR Guideline).
rationales.C: Immediate caesarean within 2 hours is wrong because immediate delivery is reserved for reversed EDF, abnormal DV (absent/reversed a-wave) or recurrent CTG decelerations; positive DV a-wave allows the 24-48h window. Would be correct only with abnormal DV a-wave or recurrent decelerations (RANZCOG: FGR Guideline).
rationales.D: Continue expectancy with repeat Dopplers in 72h is wrong because AEDF plus reduced variability is itself an indication for delivery; positive DV a-wave allows time but does not justify continued expectancy beyond 48 hours. Would be correct only at earlier surveillance phases before AEDF develops (RANZCOG: FGR Guideline).
rationales.E: Deliver at 34 weeks is wrong because AEDF at 30 weeks mandates delivery within 24-48 hours; waiting to 34 weeks risks foetal death from progressive decompensation. Would be correct only with isolated mild umbilical artery PI elevation, not AEDF (RANZCOG: FGR Guideline).

rationales.B: Platelets first is wrong because while platelets <50 plus active bleeding warrant transfusion, the immediate life-threatening defect is critically low fibrinogen, which must be corrected first to stabilise clot formation. Would be correct only after fibrinogen replacement (ANZCA Massive Transfusion Guidelines).
rationales.C: More FFP is wrong because FFP contains fibrinogen at low concentration (~2 g/L); large volumes are needed to raise fibrinogen meaningfully, risking circulatory overload. Concentrated sources (cryoprecipitate, fibrinogen concentrate) are preferred. Would be correct only as broad factor replacement when fibrinogen is adequate (RANZCOG: AFE).
rationales.D: Prothrombinex-HT is wrong because PCCs concentrate factors II, IX and X but contain negligible fibrinogen; it does not address the critical defect. Would be correct only for warfarin reversal or vitamin K-dependent factor deficiency (ANZCA Massive Transfusion Guidelines).
rationales.E: TXA only is wrong because tranexamic acid is an adjunct in PPH but cannot replace severely depleted fibrinogen; the anti-pattern is treating consumptive coagulopathy with antifibrinolytic alone. Would be correct only as adjunct alongside fibrinogen replacement (WOMAN Trial; RANZCOG: AFE).

rationales.B: Immediate hysterectomy is wrong because while hysterectomy is the definitive treatment, it sacrifices fertility; in a patient who has explicitly requested uterine conservation, leaving placenta in situ is a valid alternative when bleeding is controlled. Would be correct only with refractory bleeding or completed family (RANZCOG: PAS Guideline).
rationales.C: B-Lynch and vessel ligation is wrong because these are atony-targeted; with placenta accreta the bleeding is from placental bed invasion, not myometrial atony, so these measures cannot control it without addressing the placenta. Would be correct only for atonic PPH (RANZCOG: PAS Guideline).
rationales.D: Piecemeal removal is wrong because forced placental removal in accreta risks ongoing haemorrhage, incomplete evacuation and uterine perforation; conservative leave-in-situ approach is safer. Would never be correct as planned approach in PAS (FIGO PAS Guidelines).
rationales.E: Caesarean hysterectomy with surrogacy referral is wrong because hysterectomy itself ends uterine-mediated fertility regardless of ovarian preservation; the patient wants to keep her uterus. Would be correct only when conservation is not feasible (RANZCOG: PAS Guideline).

rationales.A: Deliver within 24 hours with rescue steroids is wrong only because the correct answer also specifies within 24 hours, but emphasises feasibility of completing at least a single dose; the option overstates rescue-course timing. (This option closely matches B in intent; the difference is that B explicitly accepts a single dose if full course is not feasible, prioritising delivery.) Would be correct only if 'rescue course' is interpreted as single dose (RANZCOG: FGR Guideline).
rationales.C: Immediate caesarean within 2 hours is wrong because immediate delivery is reserved for recurrent decelerations or abnormal DV a-wave; positive DV a-wave allows a short window for at least a single steroid dose. Would be correct only with abnormal DV a-wave or recurrent decelerations (RANZCOG: FGR Guideline).
rationales.D: Rescue course over 48h then deliver is wrong because REDF plus oligohydramnios cannot wait 48 hours; foetal death risk rises substantially. Would never be correct with REDF (RANZCOG: FGR Guideline).
rationales.E: Continued expectancy is wrong because REDF is an indication for delivery within 24 hours, not for more surveillance. Would never be correct (RANZCOG: FGR Guideline).

rationales.B: RMI 960 with watch-and-wait is wrong because the calculation is incorrect (RMI = U x M x CA-125 = 3 x 3 x 320 = 2880, not 960); furthermore, any RMI >200 warrants urgent gynae-oncology referral, not observation. Would never be correct (RANZCOG: Ovarian Masses).
rationales.C: Simple oophorectomy by general gynaecologist is wrong because high RMI requires comprehensive surgical staging by a gynaecological oncologist with appropriate intraoperative frozen section, lymphadenectomy and peritoneal sampling. Would be correct only for low-risk masses (RANZCOG: Ovarian Masses).
rationales.D: RMI 1440 and discharge to GP is wrong because the calculation is incorrect and high-risk findings require oncology referral; CT can be part of staging but never with GP discharge as the endpoint. Would never be correct (NICE CG122).
rationales.E: RMI 2880 as low risk is wrong because >200 is high risk, not low; the option misclassifies the result. Would never be correct (RANZCOG: Ovarian Masses).

rationales.B: Fundal pressure is wrong because it worsens impaction by driving the anterior shoulder onto the symphysis and risks uterine rupture and brachial plexus injury; the anti-pattern is replacing suprapubic pressure with fundal pressure. Would never be correct (RANZCOG; RCOG).
rationales.C: Symphysiotomy is wrong because it is a last-resort manoeuvre in settings where caesarean is unavailable; in Australian hospitals it precedes only Zavanelli, after all-fours and other manoeuvres have failed. Would be correct only in resource-limited settings without theatre access (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Zavanelli is wrong because cephalic replacement plus caesarean is the absolute last resort after all other manoeuvres have failed, including all-fours, and carries high foetal mortality. Would be correct only after every other manoeuvre has failed (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Repeat failed manoeuvres is wrong because the algorithm proceeds to all-fours after the first internal manoeuvre sequence has failed; repeating wastes time during hypoxia. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: Transfer to theatre is wrong because moving to theatre exceeds the 4-5 minute window; perimortem caesarean must be performed at the site of arrest if transfer cannot be completed rapidly. Would be correct only if theatre is immediately adjacent and transfer fits within the window (ANZCOR Guideline 11.6).
rationales.C: Amiodarone and defer delivery is wrong because while amiodarone is correct for refractory VF, delivery must not be delayed; both proceed in parallel to relieve aortocaval compression. Would never be correct as a substitute for delivery (ANZCOR Guideline 11.6).
rationales.D: Continue CPR with displacement 10 more minutes is wrong because waiting exceeds the 4-5 minute window and worsens maternal and foetal outcomes; the anti-pattern is using prior reassuring foetal HR to justify delay. Would never be correct (ANZCOR Guideline 11.6).
rationales.E: Tocolysis is wrong because in maternal arrest the uterus must be emptied surgically to relieve aortocaval compression, not relaxed pharmacologically; tocolysis is not indicated. Would never be correct (RANZCOG: Maternal Collapse).

rationales.A: Continue warfarin is wrong because warfarin is teratogenic between 6 and 12 weeks (warfarin embryopathy with nasal hypoplasia and chondrodysplasia punctata); furthermore she has obstetric APS without prior thrombosis, where the indication is aspirin plus LMWH, not warfarin. Would be correct only with mechanical heart valves where benefit may outweigh risk after counselling (RANZCOG; eTG).
rationales.C: Warfarin until 12 weeks then LMWH is wrong because warfarin embryopathy risk is concentrated in weeks 6-12; the switch must occur BEFORE 6 weeks, not after the teratogenic window. Would never be correct (RANZCOG; eTG).
rationales.D: Warfarin to 6 weeks then aspirin alone is wrong because aspirin monotherapy is inferior to aspirin plus LMWH in obstetric APS with recurrent loss; LMWH must be added. Would be correct only for isolated APL antibodies without obstetric criteria (RANZCOG; eTG).
rationales.E: IVIG monthly is wrong because IVIG is not first-line for obstetric APS; aspirin plus LMWH is. IVIG is reserved for refractory cases or research protocols. Would be correct only in trial settings or refractory disease (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: No antibiotics is wrong because preterm labour is itself a risk factor for early-onset GBS infection; IAP is recommended for unknown GBS status if labour occurs <37 weeks. Would be correct only at term with negative GBS swab (RANZCOG: GBS).
rationales.C: Swab and treat if positive is wrong because culture results will not be available in time to guide intrapartum decision-making; the anti-pattern is using a too-slow test as a decision tool. Would be correct only as antenatal screening, not intrapartum decision (RANZCOG: GBS).
rationales.D: Cefazolin first-line is wrong because cefazolin is reserved for non-severe penicillin allergy; with no allergy, benzylpenicillin is preferred for narrower spectrum and direct GBS activity. Would be correct only with non-severe penicillin allergy (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).
rationales.E: IM benzathine penicillin is wrong because intrapartum prophylaxis requires IV benzylpenicillin to achieve adequate placental and amniotic fluid concentrations during labour; IM benzathine is for syphilis treatment, not GBS IAP. Would never be correct for IAP (RANZCOG: GBS).

rationales.B: ICU transfer before antibiotics is wrong because the Surviving Sepsis Hour-1 bundle prioritises antibiotics within 1 hour; CVC insertion must not delay antibiotic administration. Would be correct only if peripheral access is unobtainable AND antibiotics can be given via central line within the hour (Surviving Sepsis).
rationales.C: Immediate caesarean before fluids/antibiotics is wrong because antibiotics and fluids run in parallel with theatre preparation, not sequentially; antibiotics within the first hour drive the mortality reduction. Would never be correct as a stepwise approach (Surviving Sepsis; RANZCOG).
rationales.D: IV paracetamol and wait 30 min is wrong because temperature reduction does not treat sepsis; immediate antibiotics, fluids and source control are required. Would never be correct (Surviving Sepsis).
rationales.E: CT abdomen first is wrong because the sepsis source is clinically obvious (chorioamnionitis), and CT delays antibiotics while exposing a critically unwell pregnant patient. Would be correct only when source is genuinely unclear and the patient is stable enough for imaging (Surviving Sepsis).

rationales.B: Piecemeal manual removal is wrong because forced removal of invasive placenta causes catastrophic haemorrhage and is contraindicated; the anti-pattern is treating accreta like normal retained placenta. Would never be correct (RANZCOG: PAS).
rationales.C: Close uterus and transfer for UAE is wrong because torrential haemorrhage cannot tolerate transfer to interventional radiology; immediate surgical control is needed. Would be correct only with controlled bleeding in pre-planned PAS with IR availability (RANZCOG: PAS).
rationales.D: B-Lynch and balloon tamponade is wrong because compression sutures address atony, not myometrial invasion; in accreta these are ineffective. Would be correct only for atonic PPH (FIGO PAS Guidelines).
rationales.E: TXA plus rFVIIa as definitive is wrong because medical adjuncts cannot replace surgical control of massive haemorrhage from accreta; they may help but do not substitute for surgery. Would be correct only as adjuncts alongside surgery (RANZCOG: PAS).

rationales.B: Augment with oxytocin is wrong because oxytocin augmentation in suspected rupture worsens haemorrhage and foetal compromise; the anti-pattern is treating rupture like inadequate labour. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Fundal pressure is wrong because fundal pressure in a ruptured uterus causes catastrophic extension and foetal expulsion into the peritoneum; absolutely contraindicated. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Terbutaline tocolysis is wrong because tocolysis applies to foetal distress with an intact uterus and a manageable cause (e.g. hyperstimulation); in rupture, delay worsens outcome and surgery is mandatory. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Speculum and fFN is wrong because confirmation in suspected rupture is clinical and intraoperative; further bedside testing delays surgery during a time-critical emergency. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: Oral folic acid is wrong because while folate is important antenatally for neural tube development, it does not prevent Wernicke encephalopathy when dextrose is administered to a thiamine-deficient patient. Would be correct only as routine antenatal supplementation, not as Wernicke prophylaxis (RANZCOG: Hyperemesis).
rationales.C: IV vitamin K is wrong because vitamin K addresses warfarin reversal and vitamin K-dependent factor deficiency, not Wernicke; this is a wrong-vitamin error. Would never be correct here (RANZCOG: Hyperemesis).
rationales.D: IM vitamin B12 is wrong because B12 deficiency is rare in pregnancy and is not the cause of Wernicke; thiamine (B1) is the critical vitamin. Would be correct only in confirmed B12 deficiency (RCOG Guideline 69).
rationales.E: No vitamin supplementation needed is wrong because dextrose without thiamine in a deficient hyperemesis patient can precipitate acute Wernicke encephalopathy with potentially permanent neurological damage; the anti-pattern is treating dextrose as benign. Would never be correct in significant hyperemesis (RANZCOG: Hyperemesis).

rationales.B: Immediate emergency hysterectomy is wrong because she is haemodynamically stable and wishes to preserve fertility; conservative measures are appropriate with stability. Would be correct only with uncontrolled bleeding or no fertility wish (RANZCOG: PAS).
rationales.C: Oral mifepristone is wrong because mifepristone is not part of established PAS protocols; methotrexate is the chemotherapeutic adjuvant evidenced. Would be correct only within research settings (FIGO PAS Guidelines).
rationales.D: Observation only is wrong because conservative management without adjuvant therapy in PAS placenta-in-situ carries higher delayed haemorrhage and infection risk; adjuvant methotrexate and/or UAE reduce complications. Would be correct only in selected low-risk cases under specialist follow-up (RANZCOG: PAS).
rationales.E: IV oxytocin infusion is wrong because oxytocin does not help placental involution once invasive placenta is in situ; the mechanism (myometrial contraction) is irrelevant for accreta. Would be correct only as adjunct uterotonic, not as primary involution strategy (RANZCOG: PAS).

rationales.B: Bedrest and steroids only is wrong because bulging membranes are not an absolute contraindication to rescue cerclage when there is no infection or labour; bed rest alone has poor outcomes. Would be correct only with confirmed chorioamnionitis or established labour (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Avoid indomethacin entirely is wrong because ductal closure risk applies primarily after 32 weeks; at 21+5 short-course indomethacin is acceptable and useful as tocolysis. Would be correct only at >32 weeks (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Induce labour as non-viable is wrong because at 22+0 weeks active resuscitation is increasingly offered in Australia and she wants intervention; inducing now is contrary to patient wishes and updated ANZNN guidance. Would be correct only at <22+0 weeks with palliative care chosen (ANZNN periviable consensus).
rationales.E: Abdominal cervicoisthmic cerclage is wrong because abdominal cerclage is reserved for failed transvaginal cerclage in prior pregnancies, not first-line rescue; MgSO4 neuroprotection is for >=24+0 weeks, not 21+5. Would be correct only with prior failed transvaginal cerclage (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: 39 weeks at local hospital with single obstetrician is wrong because PAS warrants planned early delivery (34-35+6) in a tertiary centre with multidisciplinary team and blood-product support; awaiting 39 weeks risks emergency haemorrhage in the late-preterm period. Would never be correct (RANZCOG; SMFM).
rationales.C: Vaginal delivery is wrong because placenta praevia with PAS is an absolute indication for caesarean; vaginal delivery would cause torrential haemorrhage from the placental site. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Deliver at 32 weeks is wrong because 32 weeks adds substantial prematurity morbidity without proportionate maternal benefit; the standard window is 34-35+6 unless acute indications arise. Would be correct only with antenatal bleeding or other acute indications (RANZCOG; SMFM).
rationales.E: Reactive delivery only at labour or distress is wrong because reactive management compromises MDT availability and increases haemorrhage mortality; PAS requires planned delivery. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: Stage I with amnioreduction is wrong because absent donor bladder and abnormal UA Doppler upgrade to at least stage III; stage I requires only oligo/polyhydramnios. Amnioreduction alone is insufficient for advanced TTTS. Would be correct only with bladder visible and normal Dopplers (Quintero classification; RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Stage II with delayed laser for steroids is wrong because the abnormal UA Doppler upgrades to stage III; steroids are not indicated at 21 weeks and delaying laser is not recommended. Would be correct only at stage II with normal Dopplers (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Stage IV with delivery at 22 weeks is wrong because stage IV requires hydrops in one or both foetuses (not described); delivery at 22 weeks is periviable with extremely high neonatal mortality. Would be correct only with hydrops and beyond-laser-window gestation (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Stage I with observation is wrong because the actual stage is III; observation is inappropriate for advanced TTTS. Would be correct only at stage I with stable surveillance (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: 1-2% mortality with shared GP/obstetrician care is wrong because Eisenmenger mortality in pregnancy is 30-50% (mWHO class IV); shared care without tertiary pulmonary hypertension input is grossly inadequate. Would never be correct (ESC Guidelines).
rationales.C: Coerced termination is wrong because while termination is medically recommended, it cannot be performed against the patient's wishes; compassionate counselling and patient-centred approach are required. Would never be correct as a coercive plan (RANZCOG: Ethics of Reproductive Choice).
rationales.D: Continue bosentan is wrong because bosentan is teratogenic and contraindicated in pregnancy; it must be replaced with pregnancy-safe alternatives (e.g. IV epoprostenol, sildenafil). Would never be correct (ESC Guidelines).
rationales.E: Cardiac surgery to close VSD is wrong because in established Eisenmenger syndrome the right-to-left shunt is protective; closure precipitates right heart failure. Would never be correct (ESC Guidelines).

rationales.B: Immediate radical hysterectomy is wrong because in second-trimester IB1, a delay of 12-18 weeks to achieve foetal maturity does not significantly worsen maternal prognosis; immediate hysterectomy sacrifices the foetus when delay is feasible. Would be correct only with rapid progression or refusal of delay (ESGO Guidelines).
rationales.C: Neoadjuvant cisplatin is wrong because neoadjuvant chemotherapy is for locally advanced disease (>=IB2), not for IB1 tumours that are amenable to primary surgical treatment. Would be correct only for >=IB2 (ESGO Guidelines).
rationales.D: LLETZ as definitive is wrong because LLETZ is inadequate for FIGO IB1; radical surgery with lymph node assessment is needed for curative treatment. Would be correct only for microinvasive (IA1) disease (ESGO Guidelines).
rationales.E: Hysterotomy delivery at 18 weeks is wrong because delivering a previable foetus is not necessary when delay to term/near-term is safe with negative nodes; this is an over-aggressive approach. Would never be correct as primary plan in IB1 (ESGO Guidelines).

rationales.A: On-ward local-anaesthetic repair with no antibiotics or follow-up is wrong because OASI requires theatre repair under regional/GA with adequate lighting and exposure; broad-spectrum antibiotics, laxatives and structured follow-up (physiotherapy, perineal clinic) are mandatory. Would never be correct (RCOG GTG 29).
rationales.B: Ward repair without anaesthesia is wrong because OASI repair requires theatre-level anaesthesia for adequate visualisation and sphincter alignment; on-ward repair without anaesthesia compromises outcome. Would never be correct (RCOG GTG 29).
rationales.D: Theatre repair but no laxative is wrong because constipation increases dehiscence and pain; lactulose for at least 10 days is standard. Narrow staphylococcal cover alone is also inadequate - broad-spectrum cover is needed. Physiotherapy and perineal clinic are part of structured follow-up. Would never be correct (RCOG GTG 29).
rationales.E: Defer repair 24h is wrong because OASI should be repaired as soon as feasible by a trained operator; delay increases infection risk. Antibiotic prophylaxis is required and laxative for at least 10 days. Would never be correct (RCOG GTG 29).

rationales.B: TSH/T4 only with empirical thyroxine is wrong because TSH/T4 alone miss the potentially fatal cortisol deficiency; giving thyroxine before replacing glucocorticoid can precipitate adrenal crisis. Would be correct only after cortisol replacement is confirmed adequate (BMJ Best Practice).
rationales.C: Random cortisol and TSH is wrong because random cortisol is unreliable due to diurnal variation; 0800 morning cortisol plus the full pituitary axis screen plus imaging is required. Would never be correct as a complete workup (Endocrine Society).
rationales.D: 24h urinary cortisol and dexamethasone suppression is wrong because these tests screen for Cushing syndrome (excess cortisol), not deficiency; the workup is in the wrong direction entirely. Would be correct only when suspecting Cushing (Endocrine Society).
rationales.E: Pelvic ultrasound and AMH is wrong because amenorrhoea in Sheehan is central (low LH/FSH and oestradiol), not primary ovarian failure; AMH would not establish the pituitary diagnosis. Would be correct only for ovarian reserve assessment, not pituitary workup (BMJ Best Practice).

rationales.B: Transfuse 2 units is wrong because she is asymptomatic at rest with stable haemodynamics and no ongoing bleeding; the NBA PBM5 restrictive threshold is Hb <70 g/L in stable patients. Would be correct only with symptomatic anaemia, ongoing bleeding or Hb <70 (NBA PBM5).
rationales.C: Oral ferrous sulphate TDS for 3 months is wrong because at this Hb level oral iron corrects slowly (months) and is poorly tolerated (GI side effects); IV iron is preferred for postpartum severe deficiency. Would be correct only for mild postpartum anaemia with good tolerance (NBA PBM5).
rationales.D: Oral ferrous fumarate TDS no follow-up is wrong because oral iron alone for severe deficiency is suboptimal and follow-up is required to confirm correction. Would never be correct (NBA PBM5).
rationales.E: ESA plus oral iron is wrong because ESAs are not routinely indicated in postpartum iron deficiency without renal disease; IV iron alone suffices. Would be correct only with renal-disease-associated anaemia not responding to iron (NBA PBM5; AMH).

rationales.A: Encourage more fluids and re-scan in 4 hours is wrong because 720 mL after 11 hours of failed voiding meets retention criteria requiring drainage; continued conservative measures risk detrusor overdistension and long-term voiding dysfunction. Would be correct only with smaller residual volume and short time since last void (KEMH; RCOG).
rationales.B: Single in-out catheter then discharge is wrong because single drainage without a structured plan risks recurrent retention; indwelling catheter for 24-48h plus structured TWOC is needed when residual >500-700 mL. Would be correct only with residual <500 mL and good spontaneous voiding (KEMH).
rationales.C: Urgent urology cystoscopy is wrong because postpartum urinary retention is typically transient and managed by obstetric teams with catheterisation and TWOC; urology referral is not first-line. Would be correct only with persistent retention after multiple failed TWOCs or suspected structural pathology (RCOG).
rationales.D: IV furosemide is wrong because furosemide treats fluid overload not retention; it would worsen the problem by increasing bladder volume further. Would never be correct in retention (KEMH).

rationales.A: Increase methyldopa is wrong because methyldopa is associated with postpartum depression and sedation and is typically weaned in the puerperium rather than uptitrated; SOMANZ prefers other agents postpartum. Would never be correct as escalation strategy (SOMANZ).
rationales.C: Atenolol is wrong because atenolol concentrates in breast milk and can cause neonatal bradycardia, hypotension and hypoglycaemia; it is avoided in breastfeeding. Would be correct only outside breastfeeding (AMH).
rationales.D: Enalapril replacing labetalol immediately is wrong because while enalapril is breastfeeding-compatible, SOMANZ prefers stepwise change with labetalol or nifedipine as first-line postpartum and methyldopa weaning pending. Would be correct only after stepwise titration of preferred agents (SOMANZ).
rationales.E: Stop all antihypertensives at >5 days postpartum is wrong because BP characteristically peaks at days 3-6 postpartum and pre-eclampsia can present or worsen up to 6 weeks postpartum; treatment continues while BP remains >=150/100. Would be correct only with sustained BP normalisation (SOMANZ).

rationales.A: Infant tongue-tie assessment is wrong because optimal latch and positioning and a normal infant suck have already been confirmed by lactation consultant assessment; a primary infant-side cause is unlikely. Would be correct only with suspected suck problems (ABA).
rationales.B: Maternal FBC and ferritin only is wrong because anaemia may contribute to fatigue but rarely causes complete failure of lactogenesis II; targeted prolactin and pituitary axis testing is needed. Would be correct only as adjunct to the targeted workup (ABA).
rationales.C: Pelvic ultrasound only is wrong because while pelvic USS helps exclude retained products, it is most useful in combination with prolactin and wider pituitary workup; alone it misses pituitary, glandular and endocrine causes. Would be correct only as part of comprehensive workup (ABA).
rationales.E: Empirical domperidone without investigation is wrong because galactagogues should follow characterisation of the underlying cause; empirical treatment risks missing Sheehan and retained products which need specific management. Would be correct only after underlying cause is established and primary low supply is confirmed (ABA; AMH).

rationales.A: Carbimazole is wrong because postpartum thyroiditis causes thyrotoxicosis by destructive release of preformed hormone, not new synthesis; antithyroid drugs (carbimazole, PTU) are ineffective. Would be correct only in Graves or other synthesis-mediated thyrotoxicosis (BMJ Best Practice; Endocrine Society).
rationales.B: Radioiodine ablation is wrong because radioiodine is for Graves disease or toxic nodular disease, not transient destructive thyroiditis; postpartum thyroiditis typically resolves spontaneously. Would never be correct here (Endocrine Society).
rationales.D: Levothyroxine is wrong because she is currently thyrotoxic (TSH <0.01, fT4 32); levothyroxine would worsen her state. Would be correct only in the subsequent hypothyroid phase or permanent hypothyroidism (Endocrine Society).
rationales.E: Lugol iodine plus dexamethasone for thyroid storm is wrong because thyroid storm requires severe features (high fever, marked tachycardia, altered consciousness, organ dysfunction) which are absent; her thyrotoxicosis is mild-to-moderate. Would be correct only with established thyroid storm (BMJ Best Practice).

rationales.A: Caesarean wound infection is wrong because the wound is intact with no erythema, fluctuance or discharge; wound infection would show cellulitis, induration, purulent discharge or seroma. Would be correct only with characteristic wound findings (RANZCOG).
rationales.B: Lactational mastitis is wrong because both breasts are soft, non-erythematous and non-tender; mastitis presents with hot, red, painful breast quadrant. Would be correct only with characteristic breast findings (ABA).
rationales.C: UTI/pyelonephritis is wrong because there is no dysuria, frequency or flank pain, urinalysis is negative for nitrites/leucocytes and renal angles are non-tender. Would be correct only with classic UTI features and positive urinalysis (Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: DVT is wrong because calves are soft, non-tender and symmetrical; while postpartum DVT does occur, bedside findings localise to uterus and lochia rather than lower limb. Would be correct only with calf swelling and other DVT features (RCOG).
